en: “On that day he created for the earth all the seeds according to their kinds and their species, and all the fruit-bearing trees and all trees of the forest. And on the fourth day he created the sun and the moon and the stars, and set them in the firmament of the heaven to give light upon all the earth.”
orig: ወበእላትው ፈጠረ ዘርእ ኩሉ ከማ ብዘርአቶሙ
title: Jubilees 2: Creation Through Angels
author: Anonymous (Enochic)

en: “And to rule over the day and the night, and to divide the light from the darkness. And God appointed the sun to be a great sign over the earth for days and for sabbaths and for months and for feasts and for years and for sabbaths of years and for jubilees and for all seasons of the years.”
orig: ወከማ ይማልእኩ እዞም መዓልት ወከማ ይሜስል
title: Jubilees 2: Creation Through Angels
author: Anonymous (Enochic)

en: And after their punishment shall have come from the Lord, the priesthood shall fail. Then shall the Lord raise up a new priest, to whom all the words of the Lord shall be revealed. And he shall execute a judgment of truth upon the earth for a multitude of days.
orig: καὶ μετὰ τὴν εκδίκησιν αὐτῶν παρὰ Κυρίου, ἐκλείψει ἡ ἱερωσύνη. τότε ἀναστήσει Κύριος ἱερέα καινόν
title: Testament of Levi 18: The New Priest
author: Anonymous (Enochic)

en: And his star shall arise in heaven, as a king shedding forth the light of knowledge in the sunshine of day, and he shall be magnified in the world. He shall shine forth as the sun in the earth, and shall remove all darkness from under heaven, and there shall be peace in all the earth.
orig: καὶ τὸ ἄστρον αὐτοῦ ἀνατελεῖ ἐν οὐρανῷ ὡς βασιλεύς
title: Testament of Levi 18: The New Priest
author: Anonymous (Enochic)

en: The heavens shall be opened, and from the temple of glory shall come upon him sanctification, with the Father’s voice, as from Abraham to Isaac. And the glory of the Most High shall be uttered over him, and the spirit of understanding and sanctification shall rest upon him in the water.
orig: οἱ οὐρανοὶ ἀνοιγήσονται, καὶ ἐκ τοῦ ναοῦ τῆς δόξης ἥξει ἐπ’ αὐτὸν ἁγιασμός
title: Testament of Levi 18: The New Priest
author: Anonymous (Enochic)

en: For he shall give the majesty of the Lord to his sons in truth forevermore. And there shall none succeed him for all generations forever. And in his priesthood the Gentiles shall be multiplied in knowledge upon the earth, and enlightened through the grace of the Lord.
orig: αὐτὸς γὰρ δώσει τὴν μεγαλωσύνην Κυρίου τοῖς υἱοῖς αὐτοῦ
title: Testament of Levi 18: The New Priest
author: Anonymous (Enochic)

en: In his priesthood sin shall come to an end, and the lawless shall cease to do evil. And he shall open the gates of paradise, and shall remove the threatening sword against Adam. And he shall give to the saints to eat from the tree of life, and the spirit of holiness shall be on them.
orig: ἐν τῇ ἱερατείᾳ αὐτοῦ ἡ ἁμαρτία ἐκλείψει, καὶ οἱ ἄνομοι παύσονται κακοποιεῖν
title: Testament of Levi 18: The New Priest
author: Anonymous (Enochic)

en: And Beliar shall be bound by him, and he shall give power to his children to tread upon evil spirits. And the Lord shall rejoice in his children, and be well pleased in his beloved ones forever. Then shall Abraham and Isaac and Jacob exult, and I will be glad, and all the saints shall clothe themselves with joy.
orig: καὶ δώσει τοῖς ἁγίοις φαγεῖν ἐκ τοῦ ξύλου τῆς ζωῆς
title: Testament of Levi 18: The New Priest
author: Anonymous (Enochic)

en: And the Lord said to Michael: “Go and take Enoch from out of his earthly garments, and anoint him with my sweet ointment, and put him into the garments of my glory.” And Michael did just as the Lord told him. He anointed me and dressed me.
orig: И рече Господь Михаилу: Поими Еноха и совлеци его ризы земныя
title: 2 Enoch 22: Enoch Transformed into an Angel
author: Anonymous (Enochic)

en: And the appearance of that ointment was greater than a great light, and its ointment was like sweet dew, and its fragrance like myrrh, shining like the sun’s ray. And I looked at myself, and I was like one of the glorious ones, and there was no observable difference.
orig: И обличие масти тоя паче света велика
title: 2 Enoch 22: Enoch Transformed into an Angel
author: Anonymous (Enochic)

en: And the Lord summoned one of his archangels by name Vreveil, who was swifter in wisdom than the other archangels, who records all the Lord’s deeds. And the Lord said to Vreveil: “Bring out the books from my storehouses, and a reed of quick-writing, and give it to Enoch, and deliver to him the choice and comforting books out of my hand.”
orig: И призва Господь единого от архангел своих именем Вревеил
title: 2 Enoch 22: Enoch Transformed into an Angel
author: Anonymous (Enochic)

en: And Vreveil hurried and brought me the books, marbled with myrrh. And he gave me the reed from his hand. And he told me all the works of heaven, earth, and sea, and all the elements, their passages and goings, and the thunderings of the thunders, the sun and moon, the goings and changings of the stars.
orig: И потщися Вревеил и принесе ми книги
title: 2 Enoch 22: Enoch Transformed into an Angel
author: Anonymous (Enochic)

en: And I wrote down all the things, and all the courses of the seasons, and their times and their names. And I wrote three hundred and sixty-six books during thirty days and thirty nights. And Vreveil was telling me and I was writing, and I did not cease writing for thirty days and thirty nights.
orig: И написах вся вещи и вся течения времен
title: 2 Enoch 22: Enoch Transformed into an Angel
author: Anonymous (Enochic)

en: For in Wisdom there is a spirit that is intelligent, holy, unique, manifold, subtle, mobile, clear, unpolluted, distinct, invulnerable, loving the good, keen, irresistible, beneficent, humane, steadfast, sure, free from anxiety, all-powerful, overseeing all, and penetrating through all spirits that are intelligent, pure, and most subtle.
orig: ἔστιν γὰρ ἐν αὐτῇ πνεῦμα νοερόν, ἅγιον, μονογενές, πολυμερές, λεπτόν, εὐκίνητον, τρανόν
title: Wisdom 7:22-8:1: Wisdom as Divine Emanation
author: Solomon (attributed)

en: For Wisdom is more mobile than any motion; because of her pureness she pervades and penetrates all things.
orig: κινητικώτερον γὰρ πάσης κινήσεως σοφία
title: Wisdom 7:22-8:1: Wisdom as Divine Emanation
author: Solomon (attributed)

en: For she is a breath of the power of God, and a pure emanation of the glory of the Almighty; therefore nothing defiled gains entrance into her. For she is a reflection of eternal light, a spotless mirror of the working of God, and an image of his goodness.
orig: ἀτμὶς γάρ ἐστιν τῆς τοῦ θεοῦ δυνάμεως καὶ ἀπόρροια τῆς τοῦ παντοκράτορος δόξης εἰλικρινής
title: Wisdom 7:22-8:1: Wisdom as Divine Emanation
author: Solomon (attributed)

en: Although she is but one, she can do all things, and while remaining in herself, she renews all things; in every generation she passes into holy souls and makes them friends of God, and prophets.
orig: μία δὲ οὖσα πάντα δύναται καὶ μένουσα ἐν αὑτῇ τὰ πάντα καινίζει
title: Wisdom 7:22-8:1: Wisdom as Divine Emanation
author: Solomon (attributed)

en: For God loves nothing so much as the person who lives with Wisdom. For she is more beautiful than the sun, and excels every constellation of the stars. Compared with the light, she is found to be superior.
orig: οὐδὲν γὰρ ἀγαπᾷ ὁ θεὸς εἰ μὴ τὸν σοφίᾳ συνοικοῦντα
title: Wisdom 7:22-8:1: Wisdom as Divine Emanation
author: Solomon (attributed)

en: For against Wisdom evil does not prevail. She reaches mightily from one end of the earth to the other, and she orders all things well.
orig: σοφίας δὲ οὐ κατισχύει κακία. διατείνει δὲ ἀπὸ πέρατος εἰς πέρας εὐρώστως
title: Wisdom 7:22-8:1: Wisdom as Divine Emanation
author: Solomon (attributed)

en: It must be understood that there are two principles: one of good and one of evil, as we shall demonstrate by authorities and clear reasons. For it is necessary to posit two principles, one of good things and one of evil things, since it is impossible that the same tree should bring forth good fruit and bad fruit.
orig: Sciendum est quod duo sunt principia: unum boni et unum mali, sicut demonstrabimus per auctoritates et rationes claras.
title: Book of the Two Principles: Cathar Dualism
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: The Lord himself says in Matthew: “A good tree cannot bring forth evil fruit, neither can a corrupt tree bring forth good fruit.” From this it is clear that there exists another principle, that of evil, which is the cause of evil things. For if there were only one principle, namely the good God, there would be no evil in the world.
orig: Dicit enim Dominus in Matthaeo: Non potest arbor bona malos fructus facere, neque arbor mala bonos fructus facere.
title: Book of the Two Principles: Cathar Dualism
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: Furthermore, the true God, being entirely good, could not have created the things we see in this world: suffering, disease, death, corruption, and the devouring of one creature by another. These things cannot proceed from a good principle. Therefore they must proceed from another principle, which is evil by nature.
orig: Praeterea verus Deus, cum sit totus bonus, non potuit creare res quas videmus in hoc mundo: dolorem, morbum, mortem, corruptionem.
title: Book of the Two Principles: Cathar Dualism
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: Now the souls of human beings are not from the evil principle but from the good God. They were created in the heavenly world, in light and in joy. But the evil principle, through envy and malice, drew them down and imprisoned them in bodies of flesh. The body is the prison of the soul. The material world is the kingdom of the evil principle.
orig: Animae autem hominum non sunt de malo principio sed de bono Deo. Creatae fuerunt in mundo caelesti, in luce et in gaudio.
title: Book of the Two Principles: Cathar Dualism
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: Christ came into this world not in true flesh but in the appearance of flesh, for the good God could not truly enter the creation of the evil principle. Christ came to reveal to souls their true origin: that they are from the good God, that the material world is not their home, and that through knowledge and the consolation of the Holy Spirit they may return to their heavenly Father.
orig: Christus venit in hunc mundum non in vera carne sed in apparentia carnis, quia bonus Deus non potuit vere intrare creaturam mali principii.
title: Book of the Two Principles: Cathar Dualism
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: Therefore the goal of the Christian life is the liberation of the soul from the body and from the material world. This is accomplished through the consolamentum, the laying on of hands by which the Holy Spirit is transmitted. The one who receives the consolamentum and keeps the commandments shall, at the death of the body, return to the kingdom of the good God, from which the soul originally came.
orig: Finis ergo vitae Christianae est liberatio animae a corpore et a mundo materiali. Hoc fit per consolamentum.
title: Book of the Two Principles: Cathar Dualism
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: I, John, your brother, who share in the tribulation and the kingdom, when I reclined at the supper on the breast of our Lord Jesus Christ, I said to him: “Lord, who is the one who will betray you?” And the Lord said to me: “The one who dips his hand with me in the dish. Then Satan will enter into him, and he will seek to betray me.”
orig: Ego Iohannes frater vester, qui particeps sum tribulationis et regni, cum recumberem in cena super pectus Domini nostri Iesu Christi.
title: Interrogatio Iohannis: The Cathar Creation Myth
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: And I said: “Lord, before Satan fell, in what glory did he stand before your Father?” And he answered me: “He was among the virtues of heaven. He had power over all things, and he was the steward of the heavenly places. He sat at the right hand of the Father, and his glory was so great that he commanded the angels of every order.”
orig: Et dixi: Domine, antequam Satanas caderet, in qua gloria stabat ante Patrem tuum?
title: Interrogatio Iohannis: The Cathar Creation Myth
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: “But he conceived a plan in his heart, wishing to place his throne above the clouds and to be like the Most High. And descending from heaven, he came down to the lower air and to the waters which were upon the face of the earth. And he said to the angel of the air and to the angel of the waters: ‘All these things are mine. If you will hear me, I will set my throne upon the clouds and will be like the Most High.’”
orig: Sed cogitavit in corde suo, volens ponere thronum suum super nubes et esse similis Altissimo.
title: Interrogatio Iohannis: The Cathar Creation Myth
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: “And descending further to the lowest waters, he considered the order of all things visible. And he took the form of a youth, and he went to the angel of the air, saying: ‘I am the son of God. Do what I command.’ And he began to create the visible world, following the pattern of the invisible world: he made the firmament, the sun, the moon, the stars, and every creature.”
orig: Et descendens ad infimas aquas, consideravit ordinem omnium rerum visibilium.
title: Interrogatio Iohannis: The Cathar Creation Myth
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: “Then he took clay from the earth and made a human body. And he commanded an angel of the second heaven to enter into the body of clay. And he took a part of it and made another body in the form of a woman, and commanded an angel of the first heaven to enter into it. And the angels wept greatly, seeing that they were clothed in mortal form.”
orig: Tunc accepit lutum de terra et fecit corpus humanum. Et iussit angelum de secundo caelo intrare in corpus luti.
title: Interrogatio Iohannis: The Cathar Creation Myth
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: “And Satan commanded them to perform the act of the flesh in the bodies of clay, and they did not know that they were committing sin. And the originator of evil devised and made a paradise, and placed the humans in it, and planted a tree in the midst of paradise, and hid his design, so that they would not know his deceit.”
orig: Et Satanas praecepit eis ut facerent opus carnis in corporibus luteis, et non cognoverunt se peccare.
title: Interrogatio Iohannis: The Cathar Creation Myth
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: Let us pray to the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. Let us pray to the Lord God, who has the power to forgive sins and to free us from the bonds of the evil one. Let us pray that the Holy Spirit may descend upon this servant of God and make this person a dwelling place of the Most High.
orig: Oremus Patrem et Filium et Spiritum Sanctum. Oremus Dominum Deum, qui habet potestatem dimittendi peccata et liberandi nos a vinculis maligni.
title: Ritual of the Consolamentum: Becoming a Perfect
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: The elder says: “Do you wish to receive the baptism of the Holy Spirit, the consolamentum of the Good Christians? Do you renounce the world and all that is in it? Do you promise to eat no meat, to take no oaths, to lie with no man or woman, and to live in truth and in the faith of the Good Christians unto the end of your days?” And the candidate answers: “I do.”
orig: Dicit senior: Vis accipere baptismum Spiritus Sancti, consolamentum Bonorum Christianorum?
title: Ritual of the Consolamentum: Becoming a Perfect
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: The elder then says: “We give to you the holy prayer which the Lord Jesus Christ taught to his disciples. Henceforth you may say this prayer at all times, by day and by night, alone and in company, and you must never eat or drink without first saying this prayer. If you fail in this, you must do penance.” And they deliver to the candidate the Pater Noster.
orig: Dicit senior: Tradimus vobis orationem sanctam quam Dominus Iesus Christus docuit discipulis suis.
title: Ritual of the Consolamentum: Becoming a Perfect
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: Then the elder places the Gospel of John upon the head of the candidate, and all the Good Christians present place their right hands upon the candidate, and the elder says: “Holy Father, receive this your servant, and send upon this person your grace and your Holy Spirit.” And they say the Pater Noster together, and the elder reads the prologue of the Gospel of John: “In the beginning was the Word.”
orig: Tunc senior ponit Evangelium Iohannis super caput candidati, et omnes Boni Christiani ponunt manus dexteras super candidatum.
title: Ritual of the Consolamentum: Becoming a Perfect
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: When the prayer and the reading are finished, the elder gives the kiss of peace to the candidate, and all the Good Christians do likewise. And from that hour the candidate is a Good Christian, a Perfect, having received the Holy Spirit through the laying on of hands, which is the true baptism that Christ gave to his apostles.
orig: Cum oratio et lectio finitae sunt, senior dat osculum pacis candidato, et omnes Boni Christiani similiter.
title: Ritual of the Consolamentum: Becoming a Perfect
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: The elder says: “You have received the Holy Spirit from God and from the Church of the Good Christians, and from us. Henceforth you are a servant of God and of Christ. You must not lie, nor swear oaths, nor touch a woman or a man in lust, nor kill any living thing, nor eat meat or eggs or cheese, nor travel alone. And if you are faithful in these things, your soul shall return to the heavenly Father when it departs this body of death.”
orig: Dicit senior: Accepistis Spiritum Sanctum a Deo et ab Ecclesia Bonorum Christianorum et a nobis.
title: Ritual of the Consolamentum: Becoming a Perfect
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: The Enlightener spoke. He said to his disciples: “Two natures existed from the beginning: the nature of Light and the nature of Darkness. They were separate from each other. The Light dwelt in the place of Light, in the great land of the Father of Greatness. The Darkness dwelt in its own land below.”
orig: ⲓⲣⲉⲥⲑⲧⲑⲩ ⲁⲥⲵⲁⲵⲉ ⲓⲉⲵⲁⲥ ⲙⲁⲥ ⲙⲁⲧⲑⲧⲑⲩ
title: Kephalaia: Light Trapped in Matter
author: Mani (attributed)

en: “The nature of Light is five greatnesses: mind, thought, insight, counsel, and resolution. And the nature of Darkness is also five: smoke, fire, wind, water, and darkness. These five elements of Darkness rose up in envy and attacked the realm of Light.”
orig: ⲧⲥⲥⲓⲩ ⲣⲉⲧⲕⲥⲕⲉⲓⲣ ⲧⲉ ⲧⲕⲥ ⲙⲉⲧⲙⲉⲕⲥⲓⲩ
title: Kephalaia: Light Trapped in Matter
author: Mani (attributed)

en: “Then the Father of Greatness called forth the Mother of Life, and the Mother of Life called forth the First Man. And the First Man armed himself with five elements—light air, light wind, light fire, light water, and light ether—and descended to the boundary to do battle with the Darkness.”
orig: ⲧⲕⲧⲉ ⲁⲥⲓⲩⲧ ⲙⲉⲧⲉⲣⲕⲩ ⲣⲉⲧⲙⲉⲑⲣⲓⲕⲣ
title: Kephalaia: Light Trapped in Matter
author: Mani (attributed)

en: “But the First Man was defeated by the Darkness, and his five light-elements were swallowed up and mixed with the five elements of Darkness. And this mixture is the substance of the visible world. The sky, the earth, all plants, all animals, and the bodies of human beings are composed of this mixture of light and darkness.”
orig: ⲁⲥⲣⲕⲧⲩ ⲇⲉ ⲁⲥⲱⲩⲣⲓⲉ ⲙⲉⲧⲕⲥⲕⲉⲓⲣ ⲣⲉⲧⲕⲕⲣⲉⲓⲉ
title: Kephalaia: Light Trapped in Matter
author: Mani (attributed)

en: “The Father of Greatness then called forth the Living Spirit, who came down and rescued the First Man from the Darkness. But the light-particles that had been swallowed could not yet be recovered. Therefore the Father created this world as a great machine for the separation of light from darkness.”
orig: ⲧⲕⲧⲉ ⲁⲥⲓⲩⲧ ⲙⲉⲧⲉⲣⲕⲩ ⲣⲉⲧⲓⲓⲁ ⲉⲧⲕⲣⲑ
title: Kephalaia: Light Trapped in Matter
author: Mani (attributed)

en: “The sun and the moon are ships of light. Each day they draw up the particles of light that have been freed from matter and carry them back to the realm of Light. And every person who lives according to the commandments of the Light—who fasts, who prays, who abstains from flesh and from the works of darkness—assists in this great work of liberation.”
orig: ⲣⲑ ⲙⲣⲑ ⲕⲥⲉ ⲣⲉⲧⲕⲥⲕⲉⲓⲣ ⲣⲉⲧⲑⲕⲩ ⲣⲉⲧⲙⲕⲕⲣⲉ
title: Kephalaia: Light Trapped in Matter
author: Mani (attributed)

en: I am in everything. I bear the heavens, I am the foundation. I support the earths, I am the light that shines forth, that gives joy to the souls. I am the life of the world: I am the milk that is in all trees. I am the sweet water that is under the children of matter.
orig: ⲁⲣⲕⲕ ⲑⲣ ⲣⲉⲧⲓⲃⲉ ⲣⲓⲙ ⲁⲓⲥⲓ ⲣⲓⲙⲉⲥⲉ ⲣⲣⲑⲥⲉ
title: Manichaean Psalm Book: I Am in Everything
author: Mani (attributed)

en: I bore these things until I had fulfilled the will of my Father. The First Man is my father. I have borne all these burdens. I came forth from his struggle. The five powers are my mother. They brought me into the world.
orig: ⲁⲓⲥⲁⲓ ⲣⲁⲓ ⲑⲣⲧⲉⲕⲩⲓ ⲣⲉⲧⲕⲥⲕⲉⲓⲣ ⲣⲉⲧⲁⲉⲓⲧ
title: Manichaean Psalm Book: I Am in Everything
author: Mani (attributed)

en: Come to me, my kinsmen, my light-givers, my limbs of light which I put on when I went to the contest. Awake, O soul, from the drunkenness in which you have been, and the sleep. Follow me to the place of the exalted land where you dwelt in the beginning.
orig: ⲁⲙⲑⲥ ⲱⲁⲣⲕⲓ ⲣⲁⲩⲥⲕⲥⲓⲣⲉ ⲣⲁⲩⲉⲓⲣⲉ ⲣⲉⲧⲕⲥⲕⲉⲓⲣ
title: Manichaean Psalm Book: I Am in Everything
author: Mani (attributed)

en: A soul cried out from the midst of the darkness. It said: “Where am I? Who has brought me to this place? I was a king’s daughter, I sat in my father’s kingdom. He sent me down to the earth, he gave me a garment of flesh. I have forgotten my royalty, I serve in the house of my captors.”
orig: ⲕⲥⲻⲥⲑ ⲁⲩⲱⲉⲱ ⲉⲃⲕⲓ ⲉⲧⲙⲉⲑⲕⲕⲣⲉ ⲁⲩⲵⲁⲥ
title: Manichaean Psalm Book: I Am in Everything
author: Mani (attributed)

en: “But my father sent his son to me. He came down to me in my lowly state. He said to me: ‘Rise up, O soul, you are from the race of kings. Look at the garment of light that awaits you. Remember the kingdom from which you have been sent. Your crown of glory is kept for you.’”
orig: ⲁⲓⲩⲧ ⲇⲉ ⲁⲥⲧⲁⲥⲓ ⲙⲉⲧⲉⲥⲑⲣⲉ ⲱⲁⲣⲕⲓ
title: Manichaean Psalm Book: I Am in Everything
author: Mani (attributed)

en: We are particles of light. We fell into the darkness, we were swallowed by death. Now we cry to the living ones, the sons of God: “Save us from the darkness, draw us up to the light, gather us into the garners of life. For we are your own members, scattered in the world.”
orig: ⲁⲣⲕⲣ ⲣⲉⲧⲕⲥⲕⲉⲓⲣ ⲁⲣⲑⲉ ⲉⲧⲙⲉⲕⲕⲣⲉ ⲁⲣⲕⲙⲕ
title: Manichaean Psalm Book: I Am in Everything
author: Mani (attributed)

en: Baptized into Christ and having put on Christ, you have been made conformable to the Son of God. For God, having predestined us for adoption, made us share the form of Christ’s glorious body. Having become partakers of Christ, you are properly called “Christs.”
orig: Εἰς Χριστὸν βαπτισθέντες καὶ Χριστὸν ἐνδυσάμενοι, συμμόρφους τοῦ Υἱοῦ τοῦ Θεοῦ γεγένησθε.
title: Catechetical Lecture 3: You Have Become Christs
author: Cyril of Jerusalem

en: Of this anointing you are called Christians, being born again through water and the Holy Spirit. For Christ was not anointed by human beings with oil or material ointment, but the Father, having appointed him Saviour of the whole world, anointed him with the Holy Spirit.
orig: Τούτου τοῦ χρίσματος Χριστιανοὶ καλεῖσθε
title: Catechetical Lecture 3: You Have Become Christs
author: Cyril of Jerusalem

en: And to you in like manner, after you had come up from the pool of the sacred waters, there was given the chrism, the antitype of that with which Christ was anointed; and this is the Holy Spirit. For as Christ after his baptism, and the descent of the Holy Spirit upon him, went forth and vanquished the adversary, so likewise with you.
orig: Καὶ ὑμῖν ὁμοίως μετὰ τὸ ἀναβῆναι ἐκ τῆς κολυμβήθρας
title: Catechetical Lecture 3: You Have Become Christs
author: Cyril of Jerusalem

en: After the anointing, you are now called “Christs,” being made worthy of this name by the anointing. For before you were deemed worthy of this grace, you had no real right to this title, but were advancing on your way toward being Christians.
orig: Μετὰ τὸ χρῖσμα οὖν Χριστοὶ καλεῖσθε
title: Catechetical Lecture 3: You Have Become Christs
author: Cyril of Jerusalem

en: Keep this chrism unspotted: for it teaches you concerning all things, if it remain in you, as you have heard the blessed John say, speaking much concerning this chrism. For this holy thing is a spiritual safeguard of the body and salvation of the soul. The ointment is applied to your forehead, and to the organs of sense: the body is anointed with the visible ointment, and the soul is sanctified by the Holy and life-giving Spirit.
orig: Τοῦτο τὸ χρῖσμα τηρήσατε ἄσπιλον
title: Catechetical Lecture 3: You Have Become Christs
author: Cyril of Jerusalem

en: And so the end of the monk and the perfection of the heart is unceasing and unbroken perseverance in prayer. And insofar as it is granted to human frailty, it strives toward immovable tranquillity of mind and perpetual purity, for the sake of which we seek all bodily labours and all contrition of heart.
orig: Finis ergo monachi et perfectionis culmen est iugis et inperturbata orationis perseverantia.
title: Conference 10: Continuous Prayer
author: John Cassian

en: Therefore whatever can lead to this end—that is, to unceasing prayer—must be pursued with all our power. This is the reason for all fasting, all vigils, all meditation on Scripture, all stripping away of possessions and renunciation of the world: so that the heart may be purified and made capable of receiving the presence of God.
orig: Quicquid ergo ad hunc finem id est ad orationem iugem perducere potest omni virtute sectandum est.
title: Conference 10: Continuous Prayer
author: John Cassian

en: And so this verse has rightly been selected from the whole body of Scripture as a formula. For it takes up all the emotions that can arise in human nature and can be adapted to every condition and every attack. It contains the invocation of God against every danger. It contains the humility of a pious confession. It contains the watchfulness of concern and of continual fear.
orig: Non inmerito ergo hic versiculus de toto scripturarum corpore tamquam formula est decerptus.
title: Conference 10: Continuous Prayer
author: John Cassian

en: This verse should be poured out in unceasing prayer, in adversity that we may be delivered, in prosperity that we may be preserved and not puffed up. Let the meditation of this verse be turned over unceasingly in your breast. Let it not leave you when you are doing any work, when you are going on a journey, when you are eating. Falling asleep, let this verse be in your thoughts; waking up, let it meet you.
orig: Hic versiculus in adversis orandus est ut erui mereamur, in prosperis ut conservemur et non extollamur.
title: Conference 10: Continuous Prayer
author: John Cassian

en: When the soul has been established in this state of tranquillity and has been freed from the meshes of all carnal desires, and the purpose of the heart has been firmly fixed on the one supreme good, then the monk will fulfil that apostolic command: “Pray without ceasing.” For the soul that has learned to pray in this manner will at last begin to pray in reality, when every intention and every thought and every word is a prayer to God.
orig: Cum ergo anima in hac tranquillitate fuerit constituta et de omnium carnalium desideriorum laqueis fuerit expedita.
title: Conference 10: Continuous Prayer
author: John Cassian

en: Then shall the prayer of the Lord be fulfilled in us: “That they all may be one, as you, Father, are in me and I in you, that they also may be one in us.” And this will happen when that perfect love of God, by which he first loved us, passes into the affection of our heart, and that prayer of our Lord shall be fulfilled: that the love with which he loved us may be in us, and we in him.
orig: Tunc implebitur in nobis illa dominica oratio: Ut omnes unum sint, sicut tu Pater in me et ego in te, ut et ipsi in nobis unum sint.
title: Conference 10: Continuous Prayer
author: John Cassian

en: Christianity is the teaching of Christ our Saviour. It is composed of the practical life, the contemplation of nature, and the contemplation of God.
orig: Χριστιανισμός ἐστιν διδασκαλία Χριστοῦ τοῦ σωτῆρος ἡμῶν πρακτικήν καὶ φυσικὴν καὶ θεολογικὴν περιέχουσα
title: Praktikos 1-3: Apatheia Leads to Love
author: Evagrius Ponticus

en: The Kingdom of Heaven is apatheia of the soul together with true knowledge of beings.
orig: Βασιλεία οὐρανῶν ἐστὶν ἀπάθεια ψυχῆς μετὰ γνώσεως τῶν ὄντων ἀληθοῦς
title: Praktikos 1-3: Apatheia Leads to Love
author: Evagrius Ponticus

en: The Kingdom of God is knowledge of the Holy Trinity, coextensive with the capacity of the intellect and surpassing its incorruptibility.
orig: Βασιλεία τοῦ Θεοῦ γνῶσίς ἐστι τῆς ἁγίας Τριάδος
title: Praktikos 1-3: Apatheia Leads to Love
author: Evagrius Ponticus

en: The proof of apatheia is had when the mind begins to see its own light, when it remains in a state of tranquillity in the presence of the images it has during sleep, and when it maintains its calm as it beholds the affairs of life.
orig: Δεῖγμα απαθείας ἐστίν, ὅταν ὁ νοῦς ἄρξηται τὸ οἰκεῖον φέγγος ὁρᾶν
title: Praktikos 1-3: Apatheia Leads to Love
author: Evagrius Ponticus

en: Apatheia is the flower of the practical life. From this flower is born the fruit of love. Love is the door to natural knowledge. Natural knowledge leads to theology and ultimate blessedness.
orig: Ἀπαθείας ἄνθος πρακτικῆς ἐστί. τούτου δὲ τοῦ ἄνθους καρπὸς ἡ ἀγάπη
title: Praktikos 1-3: Apatheia Leads to Love
author: Evagrius Ponticus

en: The state of prayer is a passionless condition, which by means of a supreme love carries off to the intelligible height the intellect that loves wisdom and that is truly spiritual.
orig: Κατάστασις προσευχῆς ἕξις ἐστὶν ἀπαθής
title: Praktikos 1-3: Apatheia Leads to Love
author: Evagrius Ponticus

en: To lose yourself in some way, as if you did not exist, and to have no awareness of yourself at all, and to be emptied out of yourself and almost annihilated—this belongs to heavenly intercourse, not to human feeling.
orig: Sic affici deificari est. Quomodo stilla aquae modica multo infusa vino deficere a se tota videtur.
title: On Loving God: The Four Degrees of Love
author: Bernard of Clairvaux

en: To be so affected is to be deified. As a small drop of water mixed with a great quantity of wine seems to lose itself entirely, taking on the taste and colour of the wine; as iron heated in the fire loses its own appearance and glows like fire; as air suffused with sunlight is transformed into the very clarity of the light, so that it seems not merely illuminated but to be the light itself—so it will be necessary for all human feeling toward the saints to melt, in some ineffable way, and to be poured entirely into the will of God.
orig: Quomodo stilla aquae modica multo infusa vino deficere a se tota videtur, dum et saporem vini induit et colorem; et quomodo ferrum ignitum et candens igni simillimum fit, pristina propriaque forma exutum; et quomodo solis luce perfusus aer in eandem transformatur luminis claritatem.
title: On Loving God: The Four Degrees of Love
author: Bernard of Clairvaux

en: For how shall God be all in all, if anything of the human remains in the human? The substance will indeed remain, but in another form, another glory, another power.
orig: Alioquin quomodo omnia in omnibus erit Deus, si in homine de homine quicquam supererit? Manebit quidem substantia, sed in alia forma, alia gloria, alia potentia.
title: On Loving God: The Four Degrees of Love
author: Bernard of Clairvaux

en: When will this happen? Who will see this? Who will possess it? “When shall I come and appear before the face of God?” O Lord my God, “my heart has said to you: my face has sought you; your face, O Lord, will I seek.” Do you think that I shall see your holy temple?
orig: Quando hoc erit? Quis hoc videbit? Quis possidebit? Quando veniam et apparebo ante faciem Dei?
title: On Loving God: The Four Degrees of Love
author: Bernard of Clairvaux

en: I do not think that can fully happen until the word is fulfilled: “Love the Lord your God with your whole heart and your whole soul and your whole strength.” Not until the heart is no longer compelled to think of the body, nor the soul to give it life and feeling, and the body’s strength, freed from weakness, is made strong in the power of God. For it is impossible to gather all these faculties and turn them toward the face of God so long as the care of this frail and wretched body demands their attention.
orig: Nec puto id posse contingere donec illud impleatur: Diliges Dominum Deum tuum ex toto corde tuo et ex tota anima tua et ex tota virtute tua.
title: On Loving God: The Four Degrees of Love
author: Bernard of Clairvaux

en: Watchfulness is a spiritual art which, if diligently practised over a long period, completely frees us, with God’s help, from impassioned thoughts, impassioned words, and evil actions. It gives us a sure knowledge of the incomprehensible God, so far as this is possible, and a solution of divine and hidden mysteries.
orig: Νῆψις ἐστὶ μέθοδος πνευματική
title: On Watchfulness: The Jesus Prayer and Stillness
author: Hesychius of Sinai

en: Watchfulness is a continual fixing and halting of thought at the door of the heart. In this way predatory and murderous thoughts are marked down as they approach and what they say and do is noted; and we can see in what way the demonic phantoms try to deceive the intellect.
orig: Νῆψίς ἐστι νοὸς προσεδρεύων τῇ θύρᾳ τῆς καρδίας
title: On Watchfulness: The Jesus Prayer and Stillness
author: Hesychius of Sinai

en: Let the name of Jesus cling to your breath, and then you will know the value of stillness (hesychia).
orig: Κολλάσθω τῇ πνοῇ σου τὸ ὄνομα τοῦ Ἰησοῦ
title: On Watchfulness: The Jesus Prayer and Stillness
author: Hesychius of Sinai

en: The heart that has been completely emptied of mental images gives birth to divine and mysterious intellections that play within it like fish and dolphins in a calm sea. The sea is fanned by a soft wind, and the heart by the Holy Spirit.
orig: Καρδία παντελῶς φαντασιῶν κεκενωμένη
title: On Watchfulness: The Jesus Prayer and Stillness
author: Hesychius of Sinai

en: Through the Jesus Prayer the intellect is purified of every image and illuminated by the divine light. The mind that has been united with the name of Jesus becomes radiant, and through this radiance the whole person is transformed into the likeness of God.
orig: Διὰ τῆς τοῦ Ἰησοῦ προσευχῆς ὁ νοῦς καθαίρεται
title: On Watchfulness: The Jesus Prayer and Stillness
author: Hesychius of Sinai

en: The name of Jesus, when clung to by the intellect and planted in the heart, fills us with the grace of the Holy Spirit. The heart that has tasted the sweetness of this name can never again be separated from it. Through it, the passions are consumed, the demons are driven out, and the divine light shines forth within the soul.
orig: Τὸ ὄνομα τοῦ Ἰησοῦ κολλώμενον τῷ νῷ
title: On Watchfulness: The Jesus Prayer and Stillness
author: Hesychius of Sinai

en: The goal of the Christian life is the acquisition of the Holy Spirit of God. Fasting, vigil, prayer, almsgiving, and every other good deed done for Christ’s sake are merely means of acquiring the Holy Spirit. Only those good deeds done for Christ’s sake bring us the fruits of the Holy Spirit.
orig: Цель жизни христианской — стяжание Духа Святого Божьего.
title: Path to Salvation: Acquisition of the Holy Spirit
author: Theophan the Recluse

en: The Holy Spirit, entering into the human being, renews and transforms the entire person. The mind is illuminated, the will is strengthened, the heart is purified. The person becomes a new creation. This is not a mere moral improvement but an ontological transformation: the person is made a participant in the divine nature.
orig: Дух Святый, входя в человека, обновляет и преображает всё существо его.
title: Path to Salvation: Acquisition of the Holy Spirit
author: Theophan the Recluse

en: The path to salvation has three stages. The first is the awakening of the soul from the sleep of sin—the moment when a person realizes that life without God is death. The second is purification: the long, painful struggle against the passions, sustained by prayer and the sacraments. The third is illumination and union: the soul, freed from the passions, is filled with divine light and participates in the life of the Holy Trinity.
orig: Путь спасения имеет три степени.
title: Path to Salvation: Acquisition of the Holy Spirit
author: Theophan the Recluse

en: Prayer is the chief means of salvation. Not prayer as the recitation of words, but prayer as the standing of the mind in the heart before God. When the mind descends into the heart and stands there in the presence of God, then true prayer begins. All other forms of prayer are preparation for this.
orig: Молитва есть главное средство спасения.
title: Path to Salvation: Acquisition of the Holy Spirit
author: Theophan the Recluse

en: When the Holy Spirit takes up permanent residence in the heart, the person enters into the state which the Fathers call theosis—deification. This does not mean that the person becomes God in essence, which is impossible, but that the person is permeated by divine grace in every faculty: mind, will, heart, and even body. The deified person is a living icon of Christ.
orig: Когда Дух Святый вселится в сердце навсегда
title: Path to Salvation: Acquisition of the Holy Spirit
author: Theophan the Recluse

en: Let no one say that theosis is only for monks or for the great saints. Every baptized person has received the seed of deification in the sacrament. The task is to nurture this seed through prayer, through the sacraments, and through the struggle against the passions. Theosis is the normal Christian life, fully realized.
orig: Никто да не скажет, что обожение только для монахов
title: Path to Salvation: Acquisition of the Holy Spirit
author: Theophan the Recluse

en: “Prayer, fasting, vigils, and all the other Christian practices, however good they may be in themselves, do not constitute the aim of our Christian life, although they serve as the indispensable means of reaching this aim. The true aim of our Christian life consists in the acquisition of the Holy Spirit of God.”
orig: Молитва, пост, бдение и всякие другие дела христианские, сколько ни хороши они сами по себе
title: Conversation with Motovilov: The Holy Spirit Testimony
author: Seraphim of Sarov

en: “But, Father Seraphim,” I said, “how can I know whether the Holy Spirit is with me or not?” Father Seraphim answered: “It is very simple, your Godliness. The Lord said: ‘Everything is simple for those who acquire understanding.’ The trouble is that we do not seek the grace of God, and in our pride of mind we do not allow it to enter our souls.”
orig: Но, батюшка, — сказал я, — как же я могу знать, что Дух Святый со мною?
title: Conversation with Motovilov: The Holy Spirit Testimony
author: Seraphim of Sarov

en: Then Father Seraphim took me firmly by the shoulders and said: “We are both now in the Spirit of God, my friend. Why do you not look at me?” I replied: “I cannot look, Father, because your face has become brighter than the sun, and my eyes ache with pain.”
orig: Тогда о. Серафим взял меня весьма крепко за плечи и сказал мне
title: Conversation with Motovilov: The Holy Spirit Testimony
author: Seraphim of Sarov

en: Father Seraphim said: “Do not be afraid, your Godliness. You too are now shining like me. You are now yourself in the fullness of the Spirit of God; otherwise you would not be able to see me as you do.” And bowing my head, I looked and was seized with holy fear. Imagine the face of a man who speaks to you from the centre of the sun, from the blinding light of its midday rays.
orig: Не устрашайтесь, ваше Боголюбие. Вы сами теперь так же светлы, как и я.
title: Conversation with Motovilov: The Holy Spirit Testimony
author: Seraphim of Sarov

en: “What do you feel?” asked Father Seraphim. “An extraordinary well-being,” I said. “But what kind of well-being?” “I feel such a calm, such peace in my soul, that I can find no words to express it.” “What else do you feel?” “An extraordinary joy in my whole heart.” “What else?” “An extraordinary warmth.” “But we are in the forest, sitting in the snow. How can you feel warmth?” “The kind of warmth you feel in a bathhouse, when they pour water on the heated stones.” “And the fragrance?” “There is nothing on earth like this fragrance.”
orig: Что же вы чувствуете? — спросил о. Серафим.
title: Conversation with Motovilov: The Holy Spirit Testimony
author: Seraphim of Sarov

en: Father Seraphim said: “Remember this, your Godliness, and remember it well: this state in which we both now find ourselves is what the Lord meant when he said, ‘The kingdom of God is within you.’ This is the grace of the Holy Spirit. This is what all the Fathers and the saints were speaking of when they described the aim and purpose of the Christian life. And the Lord has been pleased to grant this to you, a sinner, so that you may bear witness to others.”
orig: Запомните это, ваше Боголюбие, и крепко запомните.
title: Conversation with Motovilov: The Holy Spirit Testimony
author: Seraphim of Sarov

en: God made us so that we might become partakers of the divine nature, so that we might enter into eternity, and so that we might appear like him, being deified by the grace of him who begot us—through whom all that is has received its being and its coming to be.
orig: Ὁ Θεὸς πεποίηκεν ἡμᾶς ἵνα γενώμεθα θείας κοινωνοὶ φύσεως
title: 400 Chapters on Love III.25: A Deified Soul
author: Maximus the Confessor

en: A deified soul is one that, through participation in divine grace, has all the attributes that God has by nature. It has become by grace everything that God is by nature, without confusion of natures. The deified person is called “god” by grace, since God has wholly filled him.
orig: Θεοποιηθεῖσα ψυχή ἐστιν ἥτις τῇ μεθέξει τῆς θείας χάριτος πάντα ἔχει ὃσα ἔχει κατὰ φύσιν ὁ Θεός
title: 400 Chapters on Love III.25: A Deified Soul
author: Maximus the Confessor

en: God’s purpose in creating human beings was that they should become participants in his own nature, that they should be resplendent with the illumination of his grace, and that they should be perfectly united with him through the power of love.
orig: Σκοπὸς τοῦ Θεοῦ ποιήσαντος τοὺς ἀνθρώπους
title: 400 Chapters on Love III.25: A Deified Soul
author: Maximus the Confessor

en: Love is the fulfillment of all the commandments. For whoever has attained love is established in God, and God in him. If God is love, then whoever has love has God. “And God is love, and whoever abides in love abides in God, and God abides in him.”
orig: Ἀγάπη πλήρωμά ἐστι πασῶν τῶν ἐντολῶν
title: 400 Chapters on Love III.25: A Deified Soul
author: Maximus the Confessor

en: The one who loves God cannot help but love every person as himself, even though he is grieved by the passions of those who are not yet purified. But when they return and amend, his joy is indescribable and boundless.
orig: Ὁ ἀγαπῶν τὸν Θεὸν οὐ δύναται μὴ καὶ πάντα ἄνθρωπον ἀγαπᾶν ὡς ἑαυτόν
title: 400 Chapters on Love III.25: A Deified Soul
author: Maximus the Confessor

en: The human being who truly loves God prays entirely without distraction, and the one who prays without distraction loves God truly. But the one who has his mind fixed on some earthly thing does not pray without distraction; and therefore does not love God. The mind that tarries upon some earthly thing has its passions intertwined with that thing.
orig: Ὁ ἀληθῶς τὸν Θεὸν ἀγαπῶν πάντως ἀπερισπάστως προσεύχεται
title: 400 Chapters on Love III.25: A Deified Soul
author: Maximus the Confessor

en: Now we have reached the end of the journey. I have reached the summit of the Ladder. And here at the top stand three, bound together in one: faith, hope, and love. But the greatest of these is love, for God himself is called Love.
orig: Νῦν δὲ λοιπὸν τοῦ τρισσοῦ τούτου δεσμοῦ
title: Ladder of Divine Ascent, Step 30: Love Is Theosis
author: John Climacus

en: Love, by its very nature, is a resemblance to God, insofar as that is humanly possible. In its activity it is inebriation of the soul. In its distinctive properties, it is a fountain of faith, an abyss of patience, a sea of humility.
orig: Ἀγάπη κατὰ μὲν ποιότητα ὁμοίωσίς ἐστι Θεοῦ
title: Ladder of Divine Ascent, Step 30: Love Is Theosis
author: John Climacus

en: Love is the banishment of every form of contrariness, for love thinks no evil. Love, the holy state of the soul, is the door to contemplation. The person who has attained love has attained God. For “God is love.”
orig: Ἀγάπη ἐξορισμός πάσης ἐναντιότητος
title: Ladder of Divine Ascent, Step 30: Love Is Theosis
author: John Climacus

en: If someone were to ask me what love is, I would say: it is the banishment of every thought contrary to love. Love is a state of the soul that goes beyond nature and beyond words. One who has tasted it knows that it is fire. And one who has not tasted it cannot imagine what it is.
orig: Εἴ τις με ἐρωτήσειεν, τί ἐστιν ἀγάπη
title: Ladder of Divine Ascent, Step 30: Love Is Theosis
author: John Climacus

en: Love is the cause of prophecy. Love is the cause of miracles. Love is the abyss of illumination. Love is the fountain of fire: the more it gushes forth, the more the thirsty one burns with desire. Love is the establishment of angels. Love is the progress of eternity.
orig: Ἀγάπη προφητείας πρόξενος. Ἀγάπη θαυμάτων αἰτία.
title: Ladder of Divine Ascent, Step 30: Love Is Theosis
author: John Climacus

en: Tell us, O love, for we are blind without you. Tell us, O holy love, where do you pasture your flock? Where do you rest at noon? Enlighten us, quench our thirst, guide us, take us by the hand. For we wish to ascend to you at last. You rule over all. You have now conquered my soul. I cannot contain your fire. So I will go forward, singing your praises.
orig: Εἰπὲ ἡμῖν, ὦ ἀγάπη, τυφλοί γὰρ χωρὶς σοῦ
title: Ladder of Divine Ascent, Step 30: Love Is Theosis
author: John Climacus

en: The one who has reached this step, let him ask for more, if indeed there is anything beyond this. He has set his foot, while still in the flesh, in the courts of heaven. Let him now consider: is this the end, or is there still a beginning? For I believe that this is only the beginning of the light, and that the fullness of light is that which eye has not seen, and ear has not heard, and which has not entered into the heart of man.
orig: Ὁ τούτου τοῦ βαθμοῦ ἐπιβάς
title: Ladder of Divine Ascent, Step 30: Love Is Theosis
author: John Climacus

en: The Tao that can be spoken is not the eternal Tao. The name that can be named is not the eternal name.
orig: 道可道，非常道。名可名，非常名。
title: Tao Te Ching 1 + 25: The Tao That Cannot Be Spoken
author: Lao Tzu (attributed)

en: The nameless is the origin of heaven and earth. The named is the mother of the ten thousand things.
orig: 無名天地之始，有名萬物之母。
title: Tao Te Ching 1 + 25: The Tao That Cannot Be Spoken
author: Lao Tzu (attributed)

en: Therefore, always without desire, one observes its subtlety. Always with desire, one observes its manifestations. These two emerge together but differ in name. Together they are called the mystery. Mystery upon mystery—the gateway of all wonders.
orig: 故常無欲，以觀其妙。常有欲，以觀其徼。此兩者同出而異名，同謂之玄。玄之又玄，衆妙之門。
title: Tao Te Ching 1 + 25: The Tao That Cannot Be Spoken
author: Lao Tzu (attributed)

en: There was something formless and complete, born before heaven and earth. Silent and void, it stands alone and does not change, goes round and does not weary. It is capable of being the mother of the world.
orig: 有物混成，先天地生。寂公寥公，獨立而不改，周行而不殆，可以為天下母。
title: Tao Te Ching 1 + 25: The Tao That Cannot Be Spoken
author: Lao Tzu (attributed)

en: I do not know its name. I style it “Tao.” Forced to give it a name, I call it “Great.” Great means going forth. Going forth means reaching far. Reaching far means returning.
orig: 吾不知其名，字之曰道。強為之名曰大。大曰逝，逝曰遠，遠曰反。
title: Tao Te Ching 1 + 25: The Tao That Cannot Be Spoken
author: Lao Tzu (attributed)

en: Therefore the Tao is great, heaven is great, earth is great, and the king is also great. In the realm there are four great things, and the king is one of them. Humankind follows earth, earth follows heaven, heaven follows the Tao, and the Tao follows its own nature.
orig: 故道大，天大，地大，王亦大。域中有四大，而王居其一焉。人法地，地法天，天法道，道法自然。
title: Tao Te Ching 1 + 25: The Tao That Cannot Be Spoken
author: Lao Tzu (attributed)

en: The Tao produces the One. The One produces the Two. The Two produces the Three. The Three produces the ten thousand things.
orig: 道生一，一生二，二生三，三生萬物。
title: Tao Te Ching 42: The Tao Produces the One
author: Lao Tzu (attributed)

en: The ten thousand things carry yin on their backs and embrace yang. Vital breath (qi, 氣) blends these and creates harmony.
orig: 萬物負陰而抱陽，沖氣以為和。
title: Tao Te Ching 42: The Tao Produces the One
author: Lao Tzu (attributed)

en: What people detest is to be orphaned, lonely, and unworthy. Yet kings and lords call themselves by these names. Thus things are sometimes diminished by being increased, and increased by being diminished.
orig: 人之所惡，唯孤、寡、不穀，而王公以為稱。故物或損之而益，或益之而損。
title: Tao Te Ching 42: The Tao Produces the One
author: Lao Tzu (attributed)

en: What others teach, I also teach: “The violent and aggressive do not die a natural death.” I shall take this as the father of my teaching.
orig: 人之所教，我亦教之。強梁者不得其死，吾將以為教父。
title: Tao Te Ching 42: The Tao Produces the One
author: Lao Tzu (attributed)

en: Once Chuang Chou dreamed he was a butterfly, a butterfly flitting and fluttering about, happy with himself and doing as he pleased. He did not know that he was Chuang Chou.
orig: 昔者莊周夢為胡蝶，栏栏然胡蝶也，自喻適志與，不知周也。
title: Chuang Tzu: The Butterfly Dream
author: Chuang Tzu

en: Suddenly he woke up, and there he was, solid and unmistakable Chuang Chou. But he did not know if he was Chuang Chou who had dreamed he was a butterfly, or a butterfly dreaming he was Chuang Chou.
orig: 俄然覺，則蹘蹘然周也。不知周之夢為胡蝶與，胡蝶之夢為周與？
title: Chuang Tzu: The Butterfly Dream
author: Chuang Tzu

en: Between Chuang Chou and the butterfly there must be some distinction. This is called the transformation of things.
orig: 周與胡蝶，則必有分矣。此之謂物化。
title: Chuang Tzu: The Butterfly Dream
author: Chuang Tzu

en: The rivers flowing into the ocean lose their names and forms. Even so, the wise one, freed from name and form, attains the divine Person who is higher than the highest.
orig: यथा नद्यः स्यन्दमानाः समुद्रे अस्तं गच्छन्ति नामरूपे विहाय । तथा विद्वान् नामरूपाद् विमुक्तः परात्परं पुरुषमुपैति दिव्यम् ।
title: Mundaka Upanishad 3.2.9: He Who Knows Brahman Becomes Brahman
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: He who knows that supreme Brahman becomes Brahman itself. In his family, no one who does not know Brahman is born. He crosses over sorrow. He crosses over sin. Freed from the knots of the heart, he becomes immortal.
orig: स यो ह वै तत्परं ब्रह्म वेद ब्रह्मैव भवति । नास्याब्रह्मवित् कश्चन कुले भवति । तरति शोकं तरति पाप्मानं गुहाहृदयग्रन्थिभ्यो विमुक्तो’मृतो भवति ।
title: Mundaka Upanishad 3.2.9: He Who Knows Brahman Becomes Brahman
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: As it is declared in the verse: “Those who have realized the meaning of the Vedanta knowledge, who have purified their minds through the practice of renunciation—they, in the worlds of Brahman, at the end of time, are fully liberated, fully immortal.”
orig: तदेतदृचाभ्युक्तम् । वेदान्तविज्ञानसुनिश्चितार्थाः संन्यासयोगाच्छुद्धसत्त्वाः । ते ब्रह्मलोकेषु परान्तकाले परामृताः परिमुच्यन्ति सर्वे ।
title: Mundaka Upanishad 3.2.9: He Who Knows Brahman Becomes Brahman
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: I know that great Person, sun-colored, beyond darkness. Only by knowing him does one pass beyond death. There is no other path to liberation.
orig: वेदाहमेतं पुरुषं महान्तम् आदित्यवर्णं तमसः परस्तात् । तमेव विदित्वामृतमिह भवति नान्यः पन्था विद्यते’यनाय ।
title: Svetasvatara Upanishad 3.8-11: The Great Person Beyond Darkness
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: There is nothing higher than him, nothing smaller, nothing greater. He stands like a tree, rooted in heaven. By him, the Person, this whole universe is filled.
orig: यस्मात्परं नापरमस्ति किंचित् यस्मान्नाणीयो न ज्यायांश्च कश्चन । वृक्ष इव स्थाणुर्दिवि तेनेदं पूर्णं पुरुषेण सर्वम् ।
title: Svetasvatara Upanishad 3.8-11: The Great Person Beyond Darkness
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: That which is beyond this world is without form and without suffering. Those who know this become immortal. But the rest go only to sorrow.
orig: ततो यदुत्तरतरं तदरूपमनामयम् । य एतद् विदुरमृतास्ते भवन्ति इतरे दुःखमेवापियान्ति ।
title: Svetasvatara Upanishad 3.8-11: The Great Person Beyond Darkness
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: He who is in the faces, heads, and necks of all beings, who dwells in the heart of all beings, who pervades all things—he is the Lord, and he is the all-pervading, the benevolent one.
orig: सर्वाननशिरोग्रीवः सर्वभूतगुहाशयः । सर्वव्यापी स भगवांस्तस्मात् सर्वगतः शिवः ।
title: Svetasvatara Upanishad 3.8-11: The Great Person Beyond Darkness
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: Janaka said: How is knowledge to be acquired? How is liberation to be attained? And how is dispassion to be reached? Tell me this, O Lord.
orig: कथं ज्ञानमवाप्नोति कथं मुक्तिर्भविष्यति । वैराग्यश्च कथं प्राप्यः एतद् ब्रूहि प्रभो ।
title: Ashtavakra Gita 1: You Are Already Free
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: Ashtavakra said: If you desire liberation, my child, shun the objects of the senses like poison. Seek forgiveness, sincerity, kindness, contentment, and truth as you would seek nectar.
orig: मुक्तिमिच्छसि चेत्तात् विषयान् विषवत् त्यज । क्षमार्जवदयातोषसत्यं पीयूषवदमृतम् ।
title: Ashtavakra Gita 1: You Are Already Free
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: You are neither earth, nor water, nor fire, nor air, nor space. For liberation, know yourself as the witness of all these, as consciousness itself. If only you will separate yourself from the body and rest in consciousness, this very moment you will be happy, at peace, free from bondage.
orig: न पृथ्वी न जलं नाग्निर्न मारुतो नभः । एषां साक्षिणं ज्ञात्वा चिद्रूपं विमुच्यसे ।
title: Ashtavakra Gita 1: You Are Already Free
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: You do not belong to any caste or stage of life. You are not perceived by the eyes. You are unattached and formless, the witness of all things. Know this and be happy. Right and wrong, pleasure and pain, exist in the mind only, not in you. You are neither the doer nor the enjoyer. You are free—truly, you have always been free.
orig: न त्वं विप्रादिको वर्णो नाश्रमी न द्विजः । असङ्गो’सि निराकारो’सि सर्वद्रष्टा सुखी भव ।
title: Ashtavakra Gita 1: You Are Already Free
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: You are the one witness of all, and are always totally free. Your only bondage is that you see the witness as something other than this. You have been bitten by the great black serpent of ego—“I am the doer.” Drink the nectar of the faith “I am not the doer” and be happy.
orig: एको द्रष्टासि सर्वस्य त्वं प्रमुक्त एव सदा । अयमेव हि ते बन्धो द्रष्टारं पश्यस्यात्मना ।
title: Ashtavakra Gita 1: You Are Already Free
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: Burn down the forest of ignorance with the fire of the understanding: “I am the one pure awareness.” Be free from sorrow and be happy. That in which all this appears—imagined like the snake in the rope—that joy, supreme joy, awareness: that is what you are. Be happy.
orig: एकं चैतन्यमात्मानं इति निश्चयवह्निना । अज्ञानवनं दग्ध्वा सुखी भव प्रभो ।
title: Ashtavakra Gita 1: You Are Already Free
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: All the suffering in the world arises from seeking happiness for oneself. All the happiness in the world arises from seeking happiness for others.
orig: लोके यानि दुःखानि सर्वाण्यात्मसुखेच्छया । लोके यानि सुखानि सर्वाण्यन्यसुखेच्छया ।
title: Bodhicaryavatara 8: The Exchange of Self and Other
author: Shantideva

en: What need is there to say more? Look at the difference between the two: the fool who works for his own benefit, and the sage who works for the benefit of others. One who does not exchange his own happiness for the suffering of others will certainly not achieve Buddhahood—and even in the cycle of existence, there will be no joy.
orig: भाषणेन किं बहुना पश्यतामिमावयोः । स्वार्थं यः करोत्येव बालः परार्थं यः करोति स मुनिः ।
title: Bodhicaryavatara 8: The Exchange of Self and Other
author: Shantideva

en: Whatever happiness there is in the world, all comes from wanting others to be happy. Whatever suffering there is in the world, all comes from wanting oneself to be happy. But why say more? The childish work for their own benefit; the Buddhas work for the benefit of others. Just look at the difference between them!
orig: आत्मनः सुखं यत् किंचिद् यत् सर्वं परसुखेच्छया । आत्मनो दुःखं यत् किंचिद् यत् सर्वमात्मसुखेच्छया ।
title: Bodhicaryavatara 8: The Exchange of Self and Other
author: Shantideva

en: If one does not truly exchange one’s own happiness for the suffering of others, there is no attaining Buddhahood, and there is no happiness even in worldly existence.
orig: आत्मनः सुखं परदुःखेन यो न व्यतिवर्तयेत् । संसारे’पि सिद्धिर्नास्ति किमुत बोधिसुखम् ।
title: Bodhicaryavatara 8: The Exchange of Self and Other
author: Shantideva

en: Then Vimalakirti said to the assembled bodhisattvas: “Sirs, how does a bodhisattva enter the dharma-gate of non-duality? Let each speak as he understands it.”
orig: अथ विमलकीर्तिर्बोधिसत्त्वानामेतदवोचत् । कथं बोधिसत्त्वः अद्वयधर्ममुखं प्रविशति ।
title: Vimalakirti Sutra: The Thunderous Silence
author: Anonymous (Vimalakirti)

en: The bodhisattva Dharmavikata said: “Samsara and nirvana are two. But one who sees the nature of samsara sees neither arising nor ceasing, neither bondage nor liberation. Such a one enters the gate of non-duality.” The bodhisattva Shrigandha said: “‘I’ and ‘mine’ are two. Where there is no presumption of a self, there will be no possessiveness. One who is free from presumption enters the gate of non-duality.”
orig: धर्मविकतो बोधिसत्त्व आह । संसारनिर्वाणमिति द्वयम् ।
title: Vimalakirti Sutra: The Thunderous Silence
author: Anonymous (Vimalakirti)

en: Then Manjushri said: “As I understand it, when in regard to all dharmas there are no words, no speech, no indication, and no cognition, and when one has departed from all questions and answers—this is the entrance into the gate of non-duality.”
orig: मञ्जुश्रीराह । यथा मम बुद्ध्या सर्वधर्मेषु निरुक्तिरनक्षरमनाभिलापमनुद्ग्रहम् ।
title: Vimalakirti Sutra: The Thunderous Silence
author: Anonymous (Vimalakirti)

en: Then Manjushri said to Vimalakirti: “We have each given our explanations. Now, sir, it is your turn. How does the bodhisattva enter the gate of non-duality?” At this, Vimalakirti remained silent, saying nothing. Manjushri praised him, saying: “Excellent! Excellent! To have neither syllables nor speech—this is truly the entrance into the gate of non-duality.”
orig: अथ मञ्जुश्रीर्विमलकीर्तिमेतदवोचत् । विमलकीर्तिस्तूष्णीमभवत् । साधु साधु नाक्षरं न वाग्वाक् । एषा सत्यमद्वयधर्ममुखप्रवेशः ।
title: Vimalakirti Sutra: The Thunderous Silence
author: Anonymous (Vimalakirti)

en: Abraham your father rejoiced to see my day; he saw it and was glad.
orig: Ἀβραὰμ ὁ πατὴρ ὑμῶν ἠγαλλιάσατο ἵνα ἴδῃ τὴν ἡμέραν τὴν ἐμήν · καὶ εἶδεν καὶ ἐχάρη ·
title: John 8:58: Before Abraham Was, I Am
author: John (attributed)

en: The Jews therefore said to him: “You are not yet fifty years old, and you have seen Abraham?”
orig: εἶπον οὖν οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι πρὸς αὐτόν · πεντήκοντα ἔτη οὔπω ἔχεις καὶ Ἀβραὰμ ἑώρακας ·
title: John 8:58: Before Abraham Was, I Am
author: John (attributed)

en: Jesus said to them: “Truly, truly, I say to you: before Abraham was, I am.”
orig: εἶπεν αὐτοῖς Ἰησοῦς · ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν · πρὶν Ἀβραὰμ γενέσθαι ἐγὼ εἰμί ·
title: John 8:58: Before Abraham Was, I Am
author: John (attributed)

en: So they picked up stones to throw at him. But Jesus hid himself and went out of the temple.
orig: ἦραν οὖν λίθους ἵνα βάλωσιν ἐπ’ αὐτόν · Ἰησοῦς δὲ ἐκρύβη καὶ ἐξῆλθεν ἐκ τοῦ ἱεροῦ ·
title: John 8:58: Before Abraham Was, I Am
author: John (attributed)

en: For in him the whole fullness (pleroma, πλήρωμα) of deity dwells bodily,
orig: ὅτι ἐν αὐτῷ κατοικεῖ πᾶν τὸ πλήρωμα τῆς θεότητος σωματικῶς ·
title: Colossians 2:9-10: The Fullness of Deity Dwells Bodily
author: Paul (attributed)

en: and you have been filled (pepleromenoi, πεπληρωμένοι) in him, who is the head of every ruler and authority.
orig: καὶ ἐστὲ ἐν αὐτῷ πεπληρωμένοι · ὅς ἐστιν ἡ κεφαλὴ πάσης ἀρχῆς καὶ ἐξουσίας ·
title: Colossians 2:9-10: The Fullness of Deity Dwells Bodily
author: Paul (attributed)

en: Do you not know that you are God’s temple (naos, ναός) and that God’s Spirit dwells in you?
orig: οὐκ οἴδατε ὅτι ναὸς θεοῦ ἐστε καὶ τὸ πνεῦμα τοῦ θεοῦ οἰκεῖ ἐν ὑμῖν ·
title: 1 Corinthians 3:16: You Are God's Temple
author: Paul (attributed)

en: If anyone destroys God’s temple, God will destroy that person. For God’s temple is holy, and you are that temple.
orig: εἴ τις τὸν ναὸν τοῦ θεοῦ φθείρει · φθερεῖ τοῦτον ὁ θεός · ὁ γὰρ ναὸς τοῦ θεοῦ ἅγιός ἐστιν · οἵτινές ἐστε ὑμεῖς ·
title: 1 Corinthians 3:16: You Are God's Temple
author: Paul (attributed)

en: For in Christ Jesus you are all sons of God through faith. For as many of you as were baptized into Christ have put on Christ.
orig: πάντες γὰρ υἱοὶ θεοῦ ἐστε διὰ τῆς πίστεως ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ · ὅσοι γὰρ εἰς Χριστὸν ἐβαπτίσθητε · Χριστὸν ἐνεδύσασθε ·
title: Galatians 3:28: All One in Christ Jesus
author: Paul (attributed)

en: There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is neither male nor female; for you are all one in Christ Jesus.
orig: οὐκ ἔνι Ἰουδαῖος οὐδὲ ἕλλην · οὐκ ἔνι δοῦλος οὐδὲ ἐλεύθερος · οὐκ ἔνι ἄρσεν καὶ θῆλυ · πάντες γὰρ ὑμεῖς εἷς ἐστε ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ ·
title: Galatians 3:28: All One in Christ Jesus
author: Paul (attributed)

en: And if you are Christ’s, then you are Abraham’s seed, heirs according to the promise.
orig: εἰ δὲ ὑμεῖς Χριστοῦ · ἄρα τοῦ Ἀβραὰμ σπέρμα ἐστέ · κατ’ ἐπαγγελίαν κληρονόμοι ·
title: Galatians 3:28: All One in Christ Jesus
author: Paul (attributed)

en: Hear, O Israel: the Lord our God, the Lord is one.
orig: Ἄκουε Ισραηλ · Κύριος ὁ θεὸς ἡμῶν Κύριος εἷς ἐστιν ·
title: Deuteronomy 6:4-5: The Shema
author: Moses (attributed)

en: And you shall love the Lord your God with all your heart (kardia, καρδία), and with all your soul (psyche, ψυχή), and with all your strength (dynamis, δύναμις).
orig: καὶ ἀγαπήσεις Κύριον τὸν θεόν σου ἐξ ὅλης τῆς καρδίας σου καὶ ἐξ ὅλης τῆς ψυχῆς σου καὶ ἐξ ὅλης τῆς δυνάμεώς σου ·
title: Deuteronomy 6:4-5: The Shema
author: Moses (attributed)

en: Rabbi Shimon said: Woe to the person who says that the Torah merely tells stories and ordinary words. If this were so, we could compose a Torah from ordinary words, and even more excellent ones. Rather, all the words of the Torah are supernal words and supernal mysteries.
orig: אמר רבי שמעון ווי לההוא בר נש דאמר אורייתא לא אתייא אלא לאחזאה מלין בעלמא
title: Zohar I.15a: God, Torah, and Israel Are One
author: Moses de León (attributed)

en: Come and see: The Holy One, blessed be He, Torah, and Israel are all one. But these are levels upon levels, secret within secret.
orig: תא חזי קודשא בריך הוא ואורייתא וישראל כלא חד אינון אלין דרגין על דרגין סתימין בגו סתימין
title: Zohar I.15a: God, Torah, and Israel Are One
author: Moses de León (attributed)

en: The Torah is the garment of the Shekhinah, and one who gazes upon the Torah gazes upon the Shekhinah. As the soul gives life to the body, so the Torah gives life to the world.
orig: אורייתא לבושא דשכינתא ומאן דאסתכל באורייתא אסתכל בשכינתא
title: Zohar I.15a: God, Torah, and Israel Are One
author: Moses de León (attributed)

en: Know that the description of God by means of negations is the correct description—one in which there is no looseness of expression. Positive attributes, on the other hand, necessarily involve some measure of association with created things and therefore impute imperfection to God.
orig: اعلم أن وصف الله سبحانه بالسلوب هو الوصف الصحيح
title: Guide for the Perplexed I.68: What God Is Not
author: Maimonides

en: When we say God exists, we mean that his non-existence is impossible. When we say he is powerful, we mean that powerlessness is not found in him. When we say he is knowing, we mean that ignorance is not found in him. Every attribute is thus a negation of its opposite, not a positive description of the divine essence.
orig: כשאנו אומרים שהקדוש ברוך הוא קיים פירושו שהעדרו ממנו נמנע
title: Guide for the Perplexed I.68: What God Is Not
author: Maimonides

en: God’s existence is not like the existence of other things. His knowledge is not like our knowledge. His power is not like our power. The difference is not merely one of degree but of kind. The words are the same, but the meanings share nothing in common except the name.
orig: אין מציאותו כמציאות שאר הנמצאים ואין ידיעתו כידיעתנו
title: Guide for the Perplexed I.68: What God Is Not
author: Maimonides

en: Within its depth I saw that it contains, bound by love in one volume, what is scattered throughout the universe—substances and accidents and their relations, as though fused together in such a way that what I speak of is a single light.
orig:
Nel suo profondo vidi che s’interna,
legato con amore in un volume,
ciò che per l’universo si squaderna:
        sustanze e accidenti e lor costume
quasi conflati insieme, per tal modo
che ciò ch’i’ dico è un semplice lume.
title: Paradiso XXXIII: The Beatific Vision
author: Dante Alighieri

en: Within the profound and luminous substance of the exalted light, three circles appeared to me of three colours and one dimension. One seemed reflected by the other, as rainbow by rainbow, and the third seemed fire breathed equally from both. How feeble is speech, and how inadequate to my conception!
orig:
Ne la profonda e chiara sussistenza
de l’alto lume parvermi tre giri
di tre colori e d’una contenenza;
        e l’un da l’altro come iri da iri
parea reflesso, e ’l terzo parea foco
che quinci e quindi igualmente si spiri.
        Oh quanto è corto il dire e come fioco
al mio concetto!
title: Paradiso XXXIII: The Beatific Vision
author: Dante Alighieri

en: That circling which, thus conceived, appeared in you as reflected light, when my eyes had gazed upon it for a time, seemed to me painted with our likeness within itself, of its own colour; and therefore my sight was wholly fixed upon it.
orig:
Quella circulazion che sì concetta
pareva in te come lume reflesso,
da li occhi miei alquanto circunspetta,
        dentro da sé, del suo colore stesso,
mi parve pinta de la nostra effige:
per che ’l mio viso in lei tutto era messo.
title: Paradiso XXXIII: The Beatific Vision
author: Dante Alighieri

en: But already my desire and my will were being turned, like a wheel that is evenly moved, by the Love that moves the sun and the other stars.
orig:
ma già volgeva il mio disio e ’l velle,
sì come rota ch’igualmente è mossa,
l’amor che move il sole e l’altre stelle.
title: Paradiso XXXIII: The Beatific Vision
author: Dante Alighieri

en: And in this he showed me a little thing, the quantity of a hazelnut, lying in the palm of my hand, as it seemed. And it was as round as any ball. I looked upon it with the eye of my understanding, and I thought: “What may this be?” And it was answered generally thus: “It is all that is made.”
orig: And in this he shewed me a lytil thyng the quantite of a hasyl nott lyeng in the palme of my hand as me semyde. And it was as round as eny balle. I lokyd ther upon with the eye of my vnderstondyng and I thought what may this be. And it was answeryd generally thus. It is all that is made.
title: Revelations of Divine Love: The Hazelnut
author: Julian of Norwich

en: I marvelled how it might last, for I thought it might suddenly have fallen to nothing for littleness. And I was answered in my understanding: “It lasts and ever shall, for God loves it.” And so all things have their being by the love of God.
orig: I merveyled how it myght laste for me thought it myght sodenly haue fallen to nowght for lytilnes. And I was answeryd in my vnderstandyng. It lasteth and ever shall for god loueth it. And so all thyng hath the beyng be the loue of god.
title: Revelations of Divine Love: The Hazelnut
author: Julian of Norwich

en: In this little thing I saw three properties: the first is that God made it; the second is that God loves it; the third is that God preserves it. But what is that to me? Truly, the maker, the lover, and the preserver. For until I am substantially united to him, I can never have full rest nor true bliss.
orig: In this lytil thyng I saw thre propertees. The furst is that god made it. The secunde that god loueth it. The thirde that god kepeth it. But what is that to me. Sothly the maker the louer and the keper. For tylle I am substancially onyd to hym I may neuer haue full rest ne very blysse.
title: Revelations of Divine Love: The Hazelnut
author: Julian of Norwich

en: Lift up your heart unto God with a meek stirring of love; and mean himself, who made you, and bought you, and graciously called you to your state of life. And think no other thought of him. It suffices to have only a naked intent directed unto God, without any other cause than himself.
orig: Lifte vp thin herte vnto god with a meek steryng of loue and mene him self and none of his goodes. And therto loke that thou loth to think on ought bot on him self so that nought worche in thi witte ne in thi wille bot only him self.
title: The Cloud of Unknowing: He May Be Loved But Not Thought
author: Anonymous (Cloud of Unknowing)

en: For he may well be loved, but not thought. By love he may be gotten and held, but by thought never. And therefore, though it is good sometimes to think of the kindness and the worthiness of God in particular, and though this is a light and a part of contemplation, nevertheless in this work it shall be cast down and covered with a cloud of forgetting.
orig: For he may wel be louyd bot not thought. By loue may he be getyn and holden bot bi thought neither. And therfore though it be good sumtyme to think of the kyndnes and the worthines of god in specyal and though it be a light and a party of contemplacion neuertheles in this werke it schal be casten doun and coueryd with a cloude of forgetyng.
title: The Cloud of Unknowing: He May Be Loved But Not Thought
author: Anonymous (Cloud of Unknowing)

en: And step above it stoutly but with a soft demeanour, with a devout and a pleasing stirring of love, and try to pierce that darkness above you. And smite upon that thick cloud of unknowing with a sharp dart of longing love; and do not leave that work for anything that may befall.
orig: And step abouen it stalwortly bot listely with a deuoute and a plesing steryng of loue and fonde for to peerse that derknes abouen thee. And smyte apon that thicke cloude of vnknowyng with a scharpe darte of longing loue and go not thens for thing that befalleth.
title: The Cloud of Unknowing: He May Be Loved But Not Thought
author: Anonymous (Cloud of Unknowing)

en: Yan Yuan asked about humaneness (ren, 仁). The Master said: “To master oneself and return to propriety (li, 禮) is humaneness. If for one day you could master yourself and return to propriety, all under heaven would return to humaneness. Does humaneness proceed from oneself, or does it proceed from others?”
orig: 顏淵問仁。子曰：「克己復禮為仁。一日克己復禮，天下歸仁焉。為仁由己，而由人乎哉？」
title: Analects 12.1: Master Oneself and Return to Propriety
author: Confucius

en: Yan Yuan said: “May I ask about the specifics?” The Master said: “Do not look at what is contrary to propriety. Do not listen to what is contrary to propriety. Do not speak what is contrary to propriety. Do not act in a way that is contrary to propriety.”
orig: 顏淵曰：「請問其目。」子曰：「非禮勿視，非禮勿聽，非禮勿言，非禮勿動。」
title: Analects 12.1: Master Oneself and Return to Propriety
author: Confucius

en: Yan Yuan said: “Though I am not clever, I ask to devote myself to this teaching.”
orig: 顏淵曰：「回雖不敏，請事斯語矣。」
title: Analects 12.1: Master Oneself and Return to Propriety
author: Confucius

en: I am Atum, the one who made himself. I am the great god who came into being of himself, who created his names, lord of the Ennead. No god can resist me.
orig: 𓀃𓅱𓀉 𓃷𓁷 𓄯𓃻𓁷 𓁍𓁷 𓆐𓁷 𓃻𓁷
title: Coffin Text 330: I Am Atum, I Made Myself
author: Anonymous (Egyptian)

en: I am he who made myself. It was my desire that created me. It was my heart that established me. I am the one who laid the foundations of the earth, who bound together the sky. I am the one who set the gods in their places and created their offerings.
orig: 𓀃𓅱𓀉 𓄯 𓃻 𓁷 𓁍𓁷 𓅱𓀉 𓀃𓂹𓁷
title: Coffin Text 330: I Am Atum, I Made Myself
author: Anonymous (Egyptian)

en: I have come into being as Khepri. I have grown as a plant. I have disguised myself as a tortoise. I am the seed of every god. I am yesterday and I know tomorrow. I am the one who created the commands that made eternity.
orig: 𓁣𓅱𓆐 𓃻 𓁷 𓅱𓀉 𓁣𓄯𓃻
title: Coffin Text 330: I Am Atum, I Made Myself
author: Anonymous (Egyptian)

en: I have passed by on the paths which I know, to the confines of the firmament. I am the one who is not repelled among the gods. Who is he among you who shall speak against me? For I am Atum, the maker of heaven, the creator of beings, who came forth from the earth, who brought into being the seed of man.
orig: 𓀃𓅱𓀉 𓃷𓁷 𓄯𓃻𓁷 𓁍𓁷 𓅱𓀉 𓆐𓁷
title: Coffin Text 330: I Am Atum, I Made Myself
author: Anonymous (Egyptian)

en: People seek retreats for themselves—in the country, by the sea, in the mountains. You too are in the habit of longing for such things. But this is altogether the mark of a most common sort of person, when it is possible at any hour you please to retreat into yourself. For nowhere does a person retreat with more quiet or more freedom than into his own soul (psyche, ψυχή).
orig: ἀναχωρήσεις αὑτοῖς ζητοῦσιν ἄνθρωποι · ἀγροικίας · αἰγιαλούς · ὄρη · εἴωθας δὲ καὶ σὺ μάλιστα τοιαῦτα ποθεῖν · ὅλον δὲ τοῦτο ἰδιωτικώτατόν ἐστιν ·
title: Meditations IV.3: The Source of Good Within You
author: Marcus Aurelius

en: Most of all when he has within himself such things that, by gazing upon them, he is immediately in perfect ease. And by ease I mean nothing other than good order. Continually, then, grant yourself this retreat, and renew yourself. Within you there is a source of good (pege tou agathou, πηγὴ τοῦ ἀγαθοῦ) that will always spring up, if you dig.
orig: μάλιστα ὅταν τοιαῦτα ἐν ἑαυτῷ ἔχῃ τις εἰς ἃ ἐγκύψας εὐθὺς ἐν πάσῃ εὐμαρείᾳ γίνεται · συνεχῶς οὖν δίδου τοῦτο σεαυτῷ τὸ ἀναχωρεῖν καὶ ἀνανέου σεαυτόν · ἔστω δὲ πηγὴ τοῦ ἀγαθοῦ ἐν σοὶ ἀεὶ ἀναβλύζουσα ἐὰν ἀεὶ σκάπτῃς ·
title: Meditations IV.3: The Source of Good Within You
author: Marcus Aurelius

en: And remember these principles: first, that things outside the mind cannot touch the soul—they stand quiet outside, and disturbances come only from the opinion within. Second, that everything you see will change in a moment and will be no more; and constantly consider how many such changes you have already witnessed. The universe is change; life is opinion.
orig: μέμνησο δὲ τῶν δογμάτων τούτων · ὓν μὲν ὅτι τὰ πράγματα οὐχ ἅπτεται τῆς ψυχῆς · ὅ κόσμος ἀλλοίωσις · ὁ βίος ὑπόληψις ·
title: Meditations IV.3: The Source of Good Within You
author: Marcus Aurelius

en: Valentinus, having come from Egypt to Rome, attempted to construct a system of impiety from the Greek philosophers and from the so-called mystical theology. He posited a certain Depth (Bythos, Βυθός) as the first principle of all things, and alongside this Depth he placed Silence (Sige, Σιγή), whom he also called Grace and Thought.
orig: Οὗτος ἐξ Αἰγύπτου παραγενόμενος εἰς τὴν Ῥώμην, ἐπεχείρησεν ἐκ τῶν Ῥλληνικῶν φιλοσόφων καὶ τῆς καλουμένης μυστικῆς θεολογίας συστῆσαι τῆς ἀσεβείας τὸ σύστημα· Βυθόν τινα ἀρχὴν τῶν ἁπάντων ὑπέθετο, καὶ τούτῳ τῷ Βυθῷ συνυπέστησε Σιγήν, ἥν καὶ Χάριν καὶ Ἔννοιαν προσηγόρευσε.
title: Haereticarum Fabularum Compendium: On Valentinus (First English Translation)
author: Theodoret of Cyrrhus

en: From Depth and Silence he derived Mind (Nous, Νοῦς) and Truth (Aletheia, Ἀλήθεια); from these, Word (Logos) and Life (Zoe, Ζωή); from these again, Man (Anthropos, ῌΑνθρωπος) and Church (Ekklesia, Ἐκκλησία). These eight he called the first Ogdoad, the root and foundation of all things.
orig: Ἐκ Βυθοῦ καὶ Σιγῆς Νοῦν καὶ Ἀλήθειαν παρήγαγεν· ἐκ τούτων Λόγον καὶ Ζωήν· ἐκ τούτων πάλιν ῌΑνθρωπον καὶ Ἐκκλησίαν. Ταύτην τὴν ὀγδοάδα πρώτην ἀρχὴν καὶ `ρίζαν τῶν ἁπάντων προσηγόρευσε.
title: Haereticarum Fabularum Compendium: On Valentinus (First English Translation)
author: Theodoret of Cyrrhus

en: The last of the thirty aeons, Sophia (Wisdom, Σοφία), suffered a passion: she desired to comprehend the greatness of the Father without the consent of her consort. From this unlawful desire arose a formless substance, which he called the abortion of Sophia. And this passion, separated from the Pleroma, became the material from which the visible world was fashioned.
orig: ῊΗ τελευταία τῶν τριάκοντα αἰώνων Σοφία πάθος ἔπαθεν· τὸ μέγεθος τοῦ Πατρὸς καταλαβεῖν ἐπεθύμησε χωρὶς τοῦ συζύγου· ἐκ ταύτης τῆς παρανόμου ἐπιθυμίας οὐσία τις ἄμορφος προήχθη, ἥν ἔκτρωμα τῆς Σοφίας προσηγόρευσε.
title: Haereticarum Fabularum Compendium: On Valentinus (First English Translation)
author: Theodoret of Cyrrhus

en: The Demiurge, he says, fashioned this world in ignorance, not knowing the aeons above him. He imagined himself to be the only God, and said through the prophets, “I am God and there is no other.” But in truth he was merely the craftsman of matter, ignorant of the spiritual realities above him.
orig: ῊΟ Δημιουργός, φησί, τόνδε τὸν κόσμον ἐδημιούργησεν ἀγνοῶν, τοὺς ὑπερκειμένους αἰῶνας οὐκ εἰδώς· μόνον ἑαυτὸν εἶναι Θεὸν ὀιηθείς.
title: Haereticarum Fabularum Compendium: On Valentinus (First English Translation)
author: Theodoret of Cyrrhus

en: Against these fables I say: if the Father is unknowable even to the aeons that proceed from him, how can these men claim to know him through their own fabricated genealogies? The Church confesses one God, one Christ, one salvation through the Incarnation—not through elaborate mythologies but through the Word made flesh, who deifies (theopoiei, θεοποιεῖ) those who receive him in faith.
orig: Πρὸς ταῦτα φημί· εἰ ἄγνωστος ὁ Πατὴρ καὶ τοῖς ἐξ αὐτοῦ προελθοῦσιν αἰῶσι, πῶς οὗτοι διὰ τῶν οἰκείων πλασμάτων γνῶναι αὐτὸν ἐπαγγέλλονται;
title: Haereticarum Fabularum Compendium: On Valentinus (First English Translation)
author: Theodoret of Cyrrhus

en: The Holy Spirit is not among the things that are made, but among the things that are. For he gives being to those who are sanctified, not as one who himself received being from another. He who deifies (deificat) cannot himself be among the deified; he who sanctifies is above sanctification.
orig: Spiritus Sanctus non est in his quae facta sunt, sed in his quae sunt. Dat enim esse his qui sanctificantur, non tamquam ipse ab alio acceperit esse. Qui deificat, ipse inter deificatos esse non potest; qui sanctificat, supra sanctificationem est.
title: De Spiritu Sancto: Theosis Through the Spirit (First English Translation)
author: Didymus the Blind

en: When the Spirit descends upon the soul, he makes it a temple of God. What was earthly becomes heavenly; what was mortal puts on the garment of immortality. For the Spirit does not merely visit but takes up dwelling, and by dwelling he transforms the nature of the vessel into something like his own.
