orig: Cum Spiritus descendit in animam, templum Dei eam facit. Quod terrenum erat, caeleste efficitur; quod mortale erat, immortalitatis indumentum accipit. Non enim visitat tantum Spiritus, sed inhabitat, et inhabitando naturam vasis in sui similitudinem transformat.
title: De Spiritu Sancto: Theosis Through the Spirit (First English Translation)
author: Didymus the Blind

en: Through the Spirit we become partakers of the divine nature, as the Apostle Peter says. Not that we become God in essence—far from it—but that we receive through grace what God possesses by nature. The Spirit is the seal (signaculum) by which the image of God, damaged in the fall, is restored and perfected in us.
orig: Per Spiritum divinae naturae participes efficimur, ut Petrus apostolus dicit. Non quod essentia Deus efficiamur—absit—sed quod per gratiam accipiamus quod Deus natura possidet. Spiritus signaculum est quo imago Dei, in lapsu corrupta, in nobis restauratur et perficitur.
title: De Spiritu Sancto: Theosis Through the Spirit (First English Translation)
author: Didymus the Blind

en: Just as iron placed in fire does not cease to be iron but glows with the fire’s heat and light, so the soul indwelt by the Spirit does not cease to be a soul but burns with divine fire. It becomes, as it were, all light, all warmth, all God—yet remains what it was by nature, even as it becomes what it was not by grace.
orig: Quemadmodum ferrum in ignem positum non desinit esse ferrum, sed ignis calore et lumine rutilat, ita anima a Spiritu inhabitata non desinit esse anima, sed divino igne ardet. Fit quasi tota lux, tota calor, tota Deus—et tamen manet quod erat natura, dum fit quod non erat gratia.
title: De Spiritu Sancto: Theosis Through the Spirit (First English Translation)
author: Didymus the Blind

en: God said, “Let us make man in our image, after our likeness.” Observe the deliberation. He did not say “Let there be man,” as he said “Let there be light.” For light was made by command, but man was made by counsel. The creation of the world was a work of power; the creation of man was a work of wisdom and love.
orig: Εἶπεν ὁ Θεός· Ποιήσωμεν ἄνθρωπον κατ’ εἰκόνα ἡμετέραν καὶ καθ’ ὁμοίωσιν. Ὄρα τὴν βουλήν. Οὐκ εἶπεν· Γενηθήτω ἄνθρωπος, ὡς εἶπεν· Γενηθήτω φῶς.
title: Homily on Genesis 1:26: Image and Likeness (First English Translation)
author: Severianus of Gabala

en: What is the image, and what is the likeness? The image is the rational soul, the capacity for thought, the power of free choice. These belong to man by nature and cannot be destroyed—not even by sin. But the likeness is the perfection of virtue, the attainment of God-likeness through righteous living. The image is the foundation; the likeness is the building that rises upon it.
orig: Τί ἐστιν ἡ εἰκών, καὶ τί ἐστιν ἡ ὁμοίωσις; Εἰκών ἐστιν ἡ λογικὴ ψυχή, ἡ τοῦ νοεῖν δύναμις, ἡ τῆς προαιρέσεως ἐξουσία. ῊΗ δὲ ὁμοίωσις ἡ τῆς ἀρετῆς τελείωσις.
title: Homily on Genesis 1:26: Image and Likeness (First English Translation)
author: Severianus of Gabala

en: Why did God say “in our image” and not “in my image”? Because the Trinity acts together. The Father wills, the Son creates, the Spirit perfects. Man is the work of the undivided Trinity, and he bears the stamp of the Three in his very being: mind, word, and spirit—one nature in three powers, an image of the one God in three persons.
orig: Διὰ τί εἶπεν· Κατ’ εἰκόνα ἡμετέραν, καὶ οὐκ εἶπεν· Κατ’ εἰκόνα ἐμήν; Ὄτι ἡ Τριὰς ὁμοῦ ἐνεργεῖ.
title: Homily on Genesis 1:26: Image and Likeness (First English Translation)
author: Severianus of Gabala

en: The image was given; the likeness was set before us as a goal. God made us capable of becoming like him, but he did not compel us. He planted the seed; we must cultivate the harvest. This is why Scripture says “in our image” (what is given) and “after our likeness” (what is to be attained). Theosis is not a violation of nature but its fulfilment.
orig: ῊΗ εἰκών ἐδόθη· ἡ ὁμοίωσις προετέθη ὡς σκοπός. Δυνατούς ἡμᾶς ἐποίησεν ὁ Θεὸς γενέσθαι ὅμοιοι αὐτῷ, οὐκ ἠνάγκασεν.
title: Homily on Genesis 1:26: Image and Likeness (First English Translation)
author: Severianus of Gabala

en: When the Lord says, “The Father is greater than I,” he speaks not as God diminished but as man assumed. For the same one who says “I and the Father are one” also says “The Father is greater than I.” If you take both sayings from the same nature, you create a contradiction. But if you understand one from his divinity and the other from his humanity, the harmony is perfect.
orig: Ὄταν λέγῃ ὁ Κύριος· ῊΟ Πατὴρ μείζων μου ἐστίν, οὐχ ὡς Θεὸς ἐλαττωθεὶς φθέγγεται, ἀλλ’ ὡς ἄνθρωπος προσληφθείς.
title: Oration on 'The Father Is Greater' (First English Translation)
author: Amphilochius of Iconium

en: He descended that we might ascend. He called the Father greater so that we might learn the measure of our elevation. For if he who is equal to the Father became less by taking our nature, then our nature, by being taken up into him, has become greater than it was. The humility of Christ is the exaltation of humanity.
orig: Κατέβη ἵνα ἡμεῖς ἀναβῶμεν. Μείζονα τὸν Πατέρα προσεῖπεν, ἵνα μάθωμεν τὸ μέτρον τῆς ἡμετέρας ἀναβάσεως.
title: Oration on 'The Father Is Greater' (First English Translation)
author: Amphilochius of Iconium

en: Do not say that deification (theosis, θέωσις) is impossible because the Father is greater. Rather, understand that the Father’s greatness is the very reason deification is possible. For the Son did not come to make us equal to the Father—that would be madness—but to make us partakers of the divine nature through himself. As fire does not make iron into fire but makes it fiery, so God does not make man into God by nature but makes him godlike by grace.
orig: Μὴ εἴπῃς ὅτι ἡ θέωσις ἀδύνατος ἐπειδὴ ὁ Πατὴρ μείζων. Μᾶλλον νόησον ὅτι τὸ μέγεθος τοῦ Πατρὸς αἰτία τῆς θεώσεώς ἐστιν.
title: Oration on 'The Father Is Greater' (First English Translation)
author: Amphilochius of Iconium

en: He assumed what is ours so that he might give us what is his. He took servitude so that we might receive adoption. He tasted death so that we might taste immortality. This is the great exchange (antidosis, ἀντίδοσις): not that God became less, but that man became more—not in essence but in participation, not by nature but by the gift of the Spirit.
orig: Τὸ ἡμέτερον ἔλαβεν ἵνα τὸ ἑαυτοῦ δώσῃ ἡμῖν. Δουλείαν προσέλαβεν ἵνα υἱοθεσίαν ἡμεῖς λάβωμεν.
title: Oration on 'The Father Is Greater' (First English Translation)
author: Amphilochius of Iconium

en: We confess that in Christ there are two natures, divine and human, united without confusion, without change, without division, without separation. The union does not destroy the difference of the natures, nor does the difference of the natures dissolve the union. If the humanity were swallowed up by the divinity, there would be no true Incarnation—and if no Incarnation, then no salvation of our nature.
orig: ῊΟμολογοῦμεν ἐν Χριστῷ δύο φύσεις, θείαν καὶ ἀνθρωπίνην, ἀσυγχύτως, ἀτρέπτως, ἀδιαιρέτως, ἀχωρίστως ἡνωμένας.
title: Hodegos: The Two Natures and Theosis (First English Translation)
author: Anastasius of Sinai

en: The deification (theosis, θέωσις) of human nature in Christ is the model and the cause of our own deification. His flesh was deified not by being turned into divinity but by being permeated with divine energy—just as iron in fire glows with heat and light without ceasing to be iron. So also our nature, when united to God through the Spirit, receives divine properties without losing its own.
orig: ῊΗ θέωσις τῆς ἀνθρωπίνης φύσεως ἐν Χριστῷ τύπος καὶ αἰτία τῆς ἡμετέρας θεώσεώς ἐστιν.
title: Hodegos: The Two Natures and Theosis (First English Translation)
author: Anastasius of Sinai

en: Against those who say that one nature resulted from the union, we reply: if the humanity was consumed by the divinity, then humanity was not saved but destroyed. And if humanity was destroyed in Christ, then we have no hope, for he did not save what he did not assume. But if he assumed true humanity and preserved it entire in the union, then our whole nature has been brought into communion with God.
orig: Πρὸς τοὺς λέγοντας μίαν φύσιν ἐκ τῆς ἑνώσεως ἀποτελεσθῆναι φαμέν· εἰ ἡ ἀνθρωπότης ὑπὸ τῆς θεότητος κατεπόθη, οὐκ ἐσώθη ἀλλ’ ἀπώλετο.
title: Hodegos: The Two Natures and Theosis (First English Translation)
author: Anastasius of Sinai

en: A nature is not the same thing as a hypostasis. A nature indicates what something is; a hypostasis indicates that it is, and who it is. The humanity of Christ is a real, complete nature, but it does not exist as a separate hypostasis. It subsists in (enhypostatos, ἐνυπόστατος) the hypostasis of the Word.
orig: Οὐ ταὐτὸν φύσις καὶ ὑπόστασις. Φύσις μὲν τὸ τί ἐστι δηλοῖ· ὑπόστασις δὲ τὸ ὅτι ἔστι καὶ τίς ἐστιν.
title: Contra Nestorianos: Hypostatic Union and Theosis (First English Translation)
author: Leontius of Byzantium

en: Neither Nestorius nor Eutyches understood the union rightly. Nestorius divided what was united, making two persons and two sons. Eutyches confused what was distinct, making one nature where there are two. The truth is that one person, the eternal Word, assumed a complete human nature, and this human nature finds its personal existence in him—not alongside him, not absorbed into him, but in him.
orig: Οὔτε Νεστόριος οὔτε Εὐτυχὴς ὀρθῶς τὴν ἕνωσιν ἐνόησαν.
title: Contra Nestorianos: Hypostatic Union and Theosis (First English Translation)
author: Leontius of Byzantium

en: From this union we learn what our own deification means. We do not become God by nature—that belongs to the Trinity alone. But through grace and participation, human nature is capable of receiving divine properties, of being illuminated, sanctified, and made incorruptible. Christ’s humanity, united to the Word, shows that human nature can bear the divine without being annihilated. What the Word accomplished in himself by nature, the Spirit accomplishes in us by grace.
orig: Ἐκ τῆς ἑνώσεως ταύτης μανθάνομεν τί ἐστιν ἡ ἡμετέρα θέωσις.
title: Contra Nestorianos: Hypostatic Union and Theosis (First English Translation)
author: Leontius of Byzantium

en: He who sees me sees the Father. Not because I am the Father, but because the Father dwells in me so completely that to see me is to see him. And if you have seen the Father in me, then know that the same indwelling is offered to you: that the Father may dwell in you as he dwells in me, and that you too may become transparent to the divine light.
title: Homily on 'He Who Sees Me Sees the Father' (First English Translation)
author: Philoxenus of Mabbug

en: You ask whether the labour of asceticism leads only to moral improvement or to something greater. I tell you: it leads to God himself. Not merely to knowledge of God, not merely to obedience to God, but to union with God. The soul that has been purified by discipline and illuminated by prayer is made a dwelling-place of the Trinity—not by metaphor but in reality.
orig: Ἐρωτᾷς εἰ ὁ τῆς ἀσκήσεως πόνος πρὸς μόνην τὴν ἀρετὴν ἂ πρὸς μεῖζόν τι φέρει. Λέγω σοι· πρὸς αὐτὸν τὸν Θεὸν φέρει.
title: Letter on Theosis (First English Translation)
author: Nilus of Ancyra

en: Do not think that deification is reserved for the next life alone. It begins here, in this body, in this moment. When you quiet the passions and turn the mind wholly toward God, the divine light already begins to shine within you. You do not yet see it as you will see it, but it is already there—as the dawn is already the sun, though the full brightness has not yet appeared.
orig: Μὴ νομίσῃς ὅτι ἡ θέωσις μόνῃ τῇ μελλούσῃ ζωῇ ἀπόκειται. Ἔνθεν ἄρχεται, ἐν τούτῳ τῷ σώματι, ἐν τούτῳ τῷ καιρῷ.
title: Letter on Theosis (First English Translation)
author: Nilus of Ancyra

en: The deified soul does not cease to be a soul, any more than iron in fire ceases to be iron. But it acquires properties that do not belong to it by nature: it glows, it gives warmth, it shines. So the soul united to God acquires peace that surpasses understanding, joy without earthly cause, love that embraces even enemies. These are not natural human virtues but divine energies flowing through a purified vessel.
orig: ῊΗ θεωθεῖσα ψυχὴ οὐ παύεται ψυχὴ οὖσα, ὡσπερ σίδηρος ἐν πυρὶ οὐ παύεται σίδηρος ὤν.
title: Letter on Theosis (First English Translation)
author: Nilus of Ancyra

en: Baptism is not merely the remission of sins. It is the implanting of the Holy Spirit in the soul, the sowing of the divine seed, the beginning of theosis. Those who think that baptism only removes guilt have not understood the mystery. Baptism makes you a temple of God. Not potentially, not symbolically, but really.
orig: Τὸ βάπτισμα οὐ μόνον ἄφεσις ἁμαρτιῶν ἐστιν, ἀλλὰ καὶ εἰσοίκισις τοῦ ῊΑγίου Πνεύματος εἰς τὴν ψυχήν.
title: On Baptismal Theosis (First English Translation)
author: Mark the Hermit

en: Do not say: “I must become worthy before God will dwell in me.” God dwelt in you at baptism. The question is not whether he is present but whether you are aware of his presence. Asceticism does not earn the Spirit; it uncovers the Spirit who is already there. You do not light the sun; you open the shutters.
orig: Μὴ εἴπῃς· Δεῖ με ἄξιον γενέσθαι πρὶν ἢ ὁ Θεὸς ἐνοικήσῃ μοι. Ἐνὼκησεν ἐν σοὶ ἐν τῷ βαπτίσματι.
title: On Baptismal Theosis (First English Translation)
author: Mark the Hermit

en: Those who imagine that their works justify them have reversed the order. Grace came first; works follow. The tree does not produce its own root; the root produces the tree. So also: baptismal grace is the root of every good work. Without the grace already planted, no amount of effort would produce a single fruit of the Spirit.
orig: Οἱ νομίζοντες ἐξ ἔργων δικαιοῦσθαι τὴν τάξιν ἀνέστρεψαν. ῊΗ χάρις προηγεῖται· τὰ ἔργα ἕπεται.
title: On Baptismal Theosis (First English Translation)
author: Mark the Hermit

en: The whole of the spiritual life is the manifestation of what was given at baptism. You were made a child of God in the font. The rest of your life is learning to live as what you already are. Theosis is not a distant summit to be conquered; it is a gift already received, waiting to be unwrapped.
orig: Ὄλη ἡ πνευματικὴ ζωὴ φανέρωσίς ἐστι τοῦ ἐν τῷ βαπτίσματι δοθέντος.
title: On Baptismal Theosis (First English Translation)
author: Mark the Hermit

en: When the soul has been thoroughly purified by ascetical practice and has entered the state of illumination, it begins to perceive divine realities not through concepts or reasoning but through a direct sense of the mind (aisthesis tou nou, αἴσθησις τοῦ νοῦ). This is not imagination. It is the mind’s own eye, cleansed by grace, seeing what it was made to see.
orig: Ὄταν ἡ ψυχὴ διὰ τῆς πρακτικῆς καθαρθῇ τελείως καὶ εἰς τὴν τῆς φωτίσεως κατάστασιν εἰσέλθῃ, ἄρχεται αἰσθήσει τοῦ νοῦ τὰ θεῖα καταλαμβάνειν.
title: On Spiritual Knowledge: Stages of Theosis (First English Translation)
author: Diadochus of Photice

en: The first stage is purification: the stripping away of passions that cloud the mind. The second is illumination: the flooding of the purified mind with divine light. The third is perfection: the soul’s participation in God’s own life, where the distinction between knower and known begins to dissolve—not that the soul ceases to exist, but that it becomes so saturated with divine love that it can no longer find where it ends and God begins.
orig: Πρῶτον μέν ἐστιν ἡ κάθαρσις· δεύτερον ἡ φώτισις· τρίτον ἡ τελείωσις.
title: On Spiritual Knowledge: Stages of Theosis (First English Translation)
author: Diadochus of Photice

en: Spiritual knowledge is not the same as theological learning. A man may read every book and know every doctrine and still have no gnosis. For gnosis is not information about God; it is the experience of God. It is the soul tasting the divine sweetness, not merely hearing about it. Many speak of honey who have never placed it on their tongue.
orig: ῊΗ πνευματικὴ γνῶσις οὐ ταὐτόν ἐστι τῇ θεολογικῇ μαθήσει.
title: On Spiritual Knowledge: Stages of Theosis (First English Translation)
author: Diadochus of Photice

en: At the summit of spiritual knowledge, the soul becomes all flame. Not a flame that destroys but a flame that gives light and warmth to everything around it. The deified soul radiates God’s love without effort, as a lamp gives light simply by burning. This is the final stage of theosis in this life: to become, by grace, a source of divine light for others.
orig: Ἐν τῷ ἄκρῳ τῆς πνευματικῆς γνώσεως, ἡ ψυχὴ ὅλη φλὸξ γίνεται.
title: On Spiritual Knowledge: Stages of Theosis (First English Translation)
author: Diadochus of Photice

en: After An and Enlil had turned over the kingship of Ur to Nanna, at that time Ur-Nammu, son born of the goddess Ninsun, for his beloved mother who bore him—in accordance with the just laws of Utu, he established equity in the land. He expelled the dishonest, the grabbers, and the oppressors. He established the nig-si-sa (cosmic justice) in the land.
orig: 𒀀 𒌔𒃠𒂋 𒂫𒀑𒁎 𒂋𒁗𒂋 𒀑𒃵𒁎 𒁲𒂫 𒁳𒂋𒁗𒂫 𒂋𒁉𒂋 𒃡𒀒
title: Code of Ur-Nammu: The Oldest Legal Code
author: Anonymous (Sumerian)

en: He made the silver mina weigh sixty shekels. He made the shekel of silver correspond to the shekel of grain. The orphan was not delivered up to the rich man. The widow was not delivered up to the powerful man. The man of one shekel was not delivered up to the man of one mina.
orig: 𒂋𒁗 𒂫𒀑 𒀀𒁲 𒃠𒁎𒂋 𒀑𒂫 𒁳𒁗
title: Code of Ur-Nammu: The Oldest Legal Code
author: Anonymous (Sumerian)

en: If a man commits murder, that man shall be killed. If a man commits robbery, that man shall be killed. If a man detains another without lawful cause, that man shall be imprisoned and shall pay fifteen shekels of silver. If a man divorces his first wife, he shall pay her one mina of silver.
orig: 𒂋𒁗𒂋 𒁲𒂫𒁳 𒀀𒂋 𒃡𒀒𒁗
title: Code of Ur-Nammu: The Oldest Legal Code
author: Anonymous (Sumerian)

en: If a man cuts off the foot of another man, he shall pay ten shekels of silver. If a man shatters the bone of another man with a club, he shall pay one mina of silver. If a man cuts off the nose of another man with a copper knife, he shall pay two-thirds of a mina of silver.
orig: 𒂋𒁗𒂋 𒁲𒂫 𒀀𒃠 𒁎𒂋𒁗𒂫
title: Code of Ur-Nammu: The Oldest Legal Code
author: Anonymous (Sumerian)

en: The gods assembled in the council. Enki opened his mouth and addressed the great gods: “On the first, seventh, and fifteenth of the month, I will establish a purification, a bath. Let one god be slaughtered, and let the gods be purified by immersion. From his flesh and his blood let Nintu mix the clay, that god and man may be thoroughly mixed in the clay.”
orig: ilū ina puhrim ittalkamū. dÉa pî-šu īpusamma iqabbi ana ilī rabûti. ina arhi ina ūmi ešri ina ūmi hamsi šeris tallilta lu aškun ramkī. ištēn ilam linērū ilūma ina rimkisūnu lizzakkū. ina šīrisū u damisūnu dNintu lī-bal-lil ţīţţa. ilu u awelu lu šu-te-el-lu-ú ina ţīţţim.
title: Atrahasis Epic: Humanity Created from Divine Blood
author: Anonymous (Babylonian)

en: They slaughtered We-ilu, the god who had intelligence (temu), in their assembly. From his flesh and his blood Nintu mixed the clay. For the rest of time they heard the drumbeat—from the flesh of the god there was a spirit (etemmu). It made the living know its sign. So that it not be forgotten, there was a spirit.
orig: We-ilam ša išū ţēmam ina puhrisūnu inērū. ina šīrisū u damisūnu dNintu ī-bal-lil ţīţţa. ana ūm šāti uppa išmuū. ina šīr ilim etemmu ibbašši. balţū iššīšū uttassû. ana la mašēm etemmu ibbašši.
title: Atrahasis Epic: Humanity Created from Divine Blood
author: Anonymous (Babylonian)

en: After she had mixed the clay, she summoned the Anunna-gods, the great gods. The Igigi, the great gods, spat upon the clay. Mami opened her mouth and addressed the great gods: “You commanded me a task—I have completed it. You slaughtered a god together with his intelligence. I have removed your heavy work; I have imposed your toil on man.”
orig: ištu ţīţţa ī-bal-li-lu išī dAnunnakī ilī rabûti. dIgīgī ilū rabûti ru’tāsūnu ittadû ana ţīţţim.
title: Atrahasis Epic: Humanity Created from Divine Blood
author: Anonymous (Babylonian)

en: You are the sole one who made all that is, the one and only who made what exists. From whose eyes humankind came forth, and from whose mouth the gods came into being. He who makes herbage for the cattle and the fruit-tree for humankind, who makes that on which the fish live in the river and the birds in the sky.
orig: ntk wʕ ir ntt nbt, wʕ wʕ ir wnnt. ḏ pr.n rmt m irtj.fj, ḏ pr.n nr.w m rā.fj.
title: Great Hymn to Amun-Re: The Sole Creator
author: Anonymous (Egyptian)

en: The sole lord, who reaches the end of the lands each day, being one who beholds those who walk upon them. Every land chatters at his rising, every day, to praise him. Too great to be investigated, too powerful to be known. One falls immediately in a seizure of dread if his name is spoken knowingly or unknowingly.
orig: nb wʕ spr r ḏrw tāwy rʕ nb, wʕ māā šm.w ḥr.sn. tā nb ḥr mdwt n wbn.f rʕ nb r dwā.f.
title: Great Hymn to Amun-Re: The Sole Creator
author: Anonymous (Egyptian)

en: He who began coming into being in the beginning, Amun, who came into being at the beginning, whose origin is unknown. No god came into being before him. There was no other god with him to tell of his form. He had no mother who made his name. He had no father who begat him and said, “This is I.” He shaped his own egg. Power of mysterious birth, who created his own beauty. Most divine god, who came into being by himself.
orig: ẖpr m ḥāt, Imn ẖpr m ḥāt, nn rḫ ẖprw.f. nn nr ẖpr ḥāt.f.
title: Great Hymn to Amun-Re: The Sole Creator
author: Anonymous (Egyptian)

en: O nobly born, listen without distraction. At this moment, the clear light of the true nature will dawn upon you. Recognise it. O nobly born, your present awareness—this very instant of naked, luminous, empty knowing—is the dharmakaya, the body of truth. Your own awareness, naturally void, not formed as anything whatsoever, is the real.
orig: རིགས་ཀི་བུ་ཁྱོད་གསལ་བསམ་གྲལ་མ་ཡེངས་བསངས་རྒྱས་ཀི་མཐ་མ་གཉིས་བགརང་བ་བསམ་གྲལ་མ་ཡེངས།
title: Bardo Thodol: The Clear Light of Death
author: Gautama Buddha (attributed)

en: This naked awareness, bright and empty, dwelling in no particular form, is the light of Samantabhadra, the primordial Buddha. Recognising it is liberation. Not recognising it, you will wander in the six realms of existence. At this very moment, the nature of your mind is the Buddha. There is nothing to seek outside yourself.
orig: རིག་པ་གསལ་གཉིས་བགརང་བ་འདི་ཀུན་བཟང་པོ་སངས་རྒྱས་ཀི་འོད་གསལ་ཡིན།
title: Bardo Thodol: The Clear Light of Death
author: Gautama Buddha (attributed)

en: O nobly born, whatever terrifying visions may appear—wrathful deities, thundering sounds, blazing lights—recognise them all as the natural radiance of your own mind. They are not external. They are not other. They are the energy of your own awareness, appearing in forms shaped by your karma. Do not flee from them. Do not grasp at them. Rest in recognition, and you are free.
orig: རིགས་ཀི་བུ་ཁྱོད་སངས་བ་མ་ཡེངས་བསངས་རྒྱས་ཀི་མཐ་མ།
title: Bardo Thodol: The Clear Light of Death
author: Gautama Buddha (attributed)

en: Attain the utmost emptiness. Hold firm to deep stillness. The ten thousand things arise together, and I watch their return. All things flourish, and each returns to its root.
orig: 致虛極也，守靜篤也。萬物並作，吾以觀其復。夫物芸芸，各歸其根。
title: Tao Te Ching 16: Returning to the Root
author: Lao Tzu (attributed)

en: Returning to the root is called stillness. Stillness is called returning to life. Returning to life is called the constant. Knowing the constant is called clarity.
orig: 歸根曰靜，靜曰復命。復命曰常，知常曰明。
title: Tao Te Ching 16: Returning to the Root
author: Lao Tzu (attributed)

en: Not knowing the constant, one acts blindly and invites disaster. Knowing the constant, one is all-embracing. Being all-embracing, one is impartial. Being impartial, one is kingly. Being kingly, one is heavenly. Being heavenly, one is the Tao. Being the Tao, one endures. The body perishes, but there is no danger.
orig: 不知常，妄作凶。知常容，容乃公，公乃王，王乃天，天乃道，道乃久，殒身不殆。
title: Tao Te Ching 16: Returning to the Root
author: Lao Tzu (attributed)

en: The True Person of old did not oppose the few, did not boast of accomplishments, did not scheme about affairs. Being like this, he could miss the mark without regret and hit it without self-satisfaction. Being like this, he could climb the heights without trembling, enter water without getting wet, enter fire without feeling hot. His knowledge was able to ascend to the Tao—such was his level.
orig: 古之真人，不逆寡，不雄成，不謨士。若然者，過而弗悔，當而不自得也。若然者，登高不慄，入水不濡，入火不熱。是知之能登假於道者也若此。
title: Zhuangzi 6: The True Person Breathes from the Heels
author: Chuang Tzu

en: The True Person of old slept without dreaming and woke without anxiety. His food was plain and his breathing was deep. The True Person breathes from the heels; the common person breathes from the throat. The subservient gurgle out their words as if retching. Those whose desires run deep have a shallow wellspring of heaven.
orig: 古之真人，其寢不夢，其覺無憂，其食不甘，其息深深。真人之息以踵，眾人之息以喉。屈服者，其嗝言若哇。其者欲深者，其天機淺。
title: Zhuangzi 6: The True Person Breathes from the Heels
author: Chuang Tzu

en: The True Person of old did not know to love life or to hate death. He emerged without delight and returned without resistance. He came casually and went casually, and that was all. He did not forget where he began, and he did not seek where he would end. He received his body and rejoiced in it; he forgot it and returned to the source. This is called not using the mind to oppose the Tao, not using the human to assist heaven. This is called the True Person.
orig: 古之真人，不知說生，不知惡死。其出不欣，其入不距。億然而往，億然而來而已矣。不忘其所始，不求其所終。受而喜之，忘而復之。是之謂不以心损道，不以人助天。是之謂真人。
title: Zhuangzi 6: The True Person Breathes from the Heels
author: Chuang Tzu

en: Ahura Mazda spoke to Spitama Zarathustra: “When I created Mithra of the wide pastures, O Spitama, I made him as worthy of worship and prayer as I myself, Ahura Mazda. The contract-breaker destroys the whole land. He who lies to Mithra brings death upon the whole land. Never break a contract, O Spitama—neither one made with a follower of the Lie nor one made with a follower of Truth.”
orig: mrāoō ahurō mazdā spitāmāi zaraθuštrāi: yəzī him frāθərəsəm vourū-gaoyaoitiō miθrəm, spitāma, itaθa avaōhaō yazatəm daθāmī yim ahurəm mazdām.
title: Yasht 10: Hymn to Mithra
author: Zarathustra (Zoroaster)

en: We worship Mithra of the wide pastures, who is truth-speaking, eloquent, with a thousand ears, well-formed, with ten thousand eyes, lofty, wide-seeing, strong, sleepless, and ever-waking. He first of the heavenly gods reaches over the Hara mountain before the immortal sun with its swift horses. He first, in a gold-adorned form, grasps the beautiful summits and from there surveys the whole land of the Aryans.
orig:
miθrəm vourū-gaoyaoitiō yazāmaidē, haθrō-vacō hu-xšaθrō hazahra-gaošəm baevare-cašmanəm bərəzaōtəm.
yo paoiryō maināvō yazatō haraitiō parā jašaiti huvarə xšaētəm amarəxšām.
title: Yasht 10: Hymn to Mithra
author: Zarathustra (Zoroaster)

en: We worship Mithra of the wide pastures, who drives in a high-wheeled chariot made of a heavenly substance. Its one wheel is golden, fashioned in heaven. He drives forth on the right side of this wide, round earth, whose limits are far, shining like the moon among the stars, when he has put on the garment of heaven.
orig: miθrəm vourū-gaoyaoitiō yazāmaidē, yo raevō vazāiti upaōha raoxašnanəm raθəm.
title: Yasht 10: Hymn to Mithra
author: Zarathustra (Zoroaster)

en: God is the Light of the heavens and the earth. The likeness of His light is as a niche in which is a lamp. The lamp is in a glass. The glass is as it were a brilliant star.
orig: اللَّهُ نُورُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ ۖ مَثَلُ نُورِهِ كَمِشْكَاةٍ فِيهَا مِصْبَاحٌ ۖ الْمِصْبَاحُ فِي زُجَاجَةٍ ۖ الزُّجَاجَةُ كَأَنَّهَا كَوْكَبٌ دُرِّيٌّ
title: Quran 24:35: The Light Verse
author: Quran (divine revelation)

en: Fuelled from a blessed tree, an olive, neither of the East nor of the West, whose oil would almost glow even if no fire touched it. Light upon light. God guides to His light whom He wills.
orig: يُوقَدُ مِن شَجَرَةٍ مُّبَارَكَةٍ زَيْتُونَةٍ لَّا شَرْقِيَّةٍ وَلَا غَرْبِيَّةٍ يَكَادُ زَيْتُهَا يُضِيءُ وَلَوْ لَمْ تَمْسَسْهُ نَارٌ ۖ نُّورٌ عَلَىٰ نُورٍ ۖ يَهْدِي اللَّهُ لِنُورِهِ مَن يَشَاءُ
title: Quran 24:35: The Light Verse
author: Quran (divine revelation)

en: And God strikes parables for humankind. And God is the Knower of all things.
orig: وَيَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْأَمْثَالَ لِلنَّاسِ ۖ وَاللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ
title: Quran 24:35: The Light Verse
author: Quran (divine revelation)

en: Say: He is God, the One.
orig: قُلْ هُوَ اللَّهُ أَحَدٌ
title: Quran 112: God Is One
author: Quran (divine revelation)

en: God, the Eternal Refuge (as-Samad).
orig: اللَّهُ الصَّمَدُ
title: Quran 112: God Is One
author: Quran (divine revelation)

en: He begets not, nor is He begotten.
orig: لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ
title: Quran 112: God Is One
author: Quran (divine revelation)

en: And there is none comparable to Him.
orig: وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُ كُفُوًا أَحَدٌ
title: Quran 112: God Is One
author: Quran (divine revelation)
